title: El Salvador

286.6/km2 (742.3/sq mi) ([46th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density"))

Increase 0.678[[8]](#cite_note-UNHDR-10) medium ([132nd](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"))

* [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") ([USD](/wiki/ISO_4217 "ISO 4217"); since 2001) * [Salvadoran colón](/wiki/Salvadoran_col%C3%B3n "Salvadoran colón") (no longer in circulation, still legal) [[c]](#cite_note-14)
*Himno Nacional de El Salvador* "[National Anthem of El Salvador](/wiki/National_Anthem_of_El_Salvador "National Anthem of El Salvador")" | |

The official language of El Salvador is established in the Article 62 of the Constitution of 1983 to be Castilian.[[1]](#cite_note-1) 2. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is also an academic language in public schools. For more information, see [Languages of El Salvador](/wiki/Languages_of_El_Salvador "Languages of El Salvador"). 3. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** The [U.S. dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") is the main currency in use. Financial information can be expressed in U.S. dollars and in [Salvadoran colóns](/wiki/Salvadoran_col%C3%B3n "Salvadoran colón"), but the colón is out of circulation.[[9]](#cite_note-integracion/ley-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12) [Bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") was [designated as legal tender in 2021](/wiki/Bitcoin_in_El_Salvador "Bitcoin in El Salvador"), but is no longer legal tender.[[11]](#cite_note-bbc.com-13) 4. **[^](#cite_ref-15)**  Telephone companies (market share): Tigo (45%), Claro (25%), Movistar (24%), Digicel (5.5%), Red (0.5%). | |

**El Salvador**,[[a]](#cite_note-16) officially the **Republic of El Salvador**,[[b]](#cite_note-17) is a country in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"). It is bordered on the northeast by [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), on the northwest by [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), and on the south by the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). El Salvador's capital and largest city is [San Salvador](/wiki/San_Salvador "San Salvador"). El Salvador's population in 2024 was estimated to be 6 million.[[5]](#cite_note-census-7)

Among the [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") nations that historically controlled the region are the [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples")[[12]](#cite_note-18) and then the [Cuzcatlecs](/wiki/Cuzcatlan "Cuzcatlan").[[13]](#cite_note-19) Archaeological monuments also suggest an early [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec") presence around the first millennium BC.[[14]](#cite_note-20) In the beginning of the 16th century, the [Spanish Empire conquered](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_El_Salvador "Spanish conquest of El Salvador") the Central American territory, incorporating it into the Viceroyalty of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") ruled from [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"). However, the Viceroyalty of New Spain had little to no influence in the daily affairs of the isthmus, which was colonized in 1524. In 1609, the area was declared the [Captaincy General of Guatemala](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Guatemala "Captaincy General of Guatemala") by the Spanish, which included the territory that would become El Salvador until its independence from Spain in 1821. It was [forcibly incorporated](/wiki/Central_America_under_Mexican_rule "Central America under Mexican rule") into the [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire"), then seceded, joining the [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America") in 1823. When the federation dissolved in 1841, El Salvador became a sovereign state. It then formed a short-lived union with Honduras and [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), called the [Greater Republic of Central America](/wiki/Greater_Republic_of_Central_America "Greater Republic of Central America"), which lasted from 1896 to 1898.[[15]](#cite_note-Boland2001-21)[[16]](#cite_note-IhrieOropesa2011-22)[[17]](#cite_note-Haskin2012-23)

From the late 19th to the mid-20th century, El Salvador endured chronic political and economic instability characterized by coups, revolts, and a succession of authoritarian rulers. Persistent socioeconomic inequality and civil unrest culminated in the [Salvadoran Civil War](/wiki/Salvadoran_Civil_War "Salvadoran Civil War") from 1979 to 1992, fought between the military-led government and a coalition of left-wing [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") groups. The conflict ended with the [Chapultepec Peace Accords](/wiki/Chapultepec_Peace_Accords "Chapultepec Peace Accords"). This negotiated settlement established a multiparty constitutional republic, which remains in place to this day.
During the civil war and afterwards, large numbers of Salvadorans emigrated to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). From 1980 to 2008, nearly one million Salvadorans emigrated to the United States, such that by 2008, they were the sixth largest immigrant group in the US.[[18]](#cite_note-migrationPolicy2010-24)

The [economy of El Salvador](/wiki/Economy_of_El_Salvador "Economy of El Salvador") has historically been dominated by agriculture, beginning with the Spanish taking control of the indigenous [cacao](/wiki/Cacao_bean "Cacao bean") crop in the 16th century, with production centred in [Izalco](/wiki/Izalco "Izalco"), along with balsam from the ranges of [La Libertad](/wiki/La_Libertad_Department_(El_Salvador) "La Libertad Department (El Salvador)") and [Ahuachapán](/wiki/Ahuachap%C3%A1n_Department "Ahuachapán Department"). This was followed by a boom in use of the [indigo plant](/wiki/Indigofera "Indigofera") in the 19th century, mainly for its use as a dye.[[19]](#cite_note-Montgomery1995-25)[[20]](#cite_note-Murray1997-26) Thereafter the focus shifted to [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), which by the early 20th century accounted for 90% of export earnings.[[21]](#cite_note-Boland2001b-27)[[22]](#cite_note-Pearcy2006-28) El Salvador has since reduced its dependence on coffee and embarked on diversifying its economy by opening up trade and financial links and expanding the manufacturing sector.[[23]](#cite_note-FoleyHapipi2005-29) The [colón](/wiki/Salvadoran_col%C3%B3n "Salvadoran colón"), the currency of El Salvador since 1892, was replaced by the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") in 2001.[[9]](#cite_note-integracion/ley-11) As of 2019, Salvadoran economics gifted them the lowest level of [income inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") out of nearby countries. Among 77 countries included in a 2021 study, El Salvador had one of the least complex economies for doing business.[[24]](#cite_note-30)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

After the [Spanish conquest](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_El_Salvador "Spanish conquest of El Salvador"), the land was divided into the [Province of San Salvador](/wiki/Province_of_San_Salvador "Province of San Salvador"), the Spanish version of "Holy Saviour", a biblical title for [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"). From 1579 it included the province of [San Miguel](/wiki/San_Miguel,_El_Salvador "San Miguel, El Salvador") (Saint Michael). Throughout the colonial era, El Salvador was an *alcaldía mayor* ('great mayor's office'), an [intendancy](/wiki/Intendancy_of_San_Salvador "Intendancy of San Salvador"), a province with a provincial council, and the province of Izalco; later called the mayor's office of [Sonsonate](/wiki/Sonsonate,_El_Salvador "Sonsonate, El Salvador"). In 1824, the two jurisdictions were united in the State of Salvador, a part of the [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America").[[25]](#cite_note-el_nombre_El_Salvador-31)[[26]](#cite_note-Ejercicio_gobernar-32)

After the dissolution of the Federal Republic of Central America, the country was referred to as the *Republic of Salvador* (*República del Salvador*). In 1915, the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador") passed a law which stated that the country's name should be rendered as the definite form *El Salvador* ('The Saviour') — again a reference to Jesus — rather than *Salvador*. With another law passed in 1958, the legislature reaffirmed the country's name as *El Salvador*.[[27]](#cite_note-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of El Salvador](/wiki/History_of_El_Salvador "History of El Salvador")

### Prehistoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistoric")]

During the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") El Salvador was inhabited by now extinct [megafauna](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") species, including the elephant-sized giant ground sloth *[Eremotherium](/wiki/Eremotherium "Eremotherium")*, the rhinoceros-like *[Mixotoxodon](/wiki/Mixotoxodon "Mixotoxodon")*, the [gomphothere](/wiki/Gomphothere "Gomphothere") (elephant-relative) *[Cuvieronius](/wiki/Cuvieronius "Cuvieronius")*, the [glyptodont](/wiki/Glyptodont "Glyptodont") *[Glyptotherium](/wiki/Glyptotherium "Glyptotherium")*, the llama *[Hemiauchenia](/wiki/Hemiauchenia "Hemiauchenia")*, and the horse *[Equus conversidens](/wiki/Equus_conversidens "Equus conversidens")*.[[28]](#cite_note-34)[[29]](#cite_note-35) El Salvador has likely been occupied by humans since the [Paleoindian](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") period, based on fluted stone points found in western El Salvador.[[30]](#cite_note-36)

### Pre-Columbian era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pre-Columbian era")]

Archaeological knowledge of Pre-Columbian civilization in El Salvador is poor, due to its high population density limiting excavation, as well as volcanic eruptions blanketing potential archaeological sites. This lack of knowledge particularly affects the [Preclassic Period](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology#Preclassic_Era_or_Formative_Period "Mesoamerican chronology") and earlier.[[31]](#cite_note-37)

A notable archaeological site in western El Salvador is [Chalchuapa](/wiki/Chalchuapa "Chalchuapa"), which was first settled around 1200 BC, and became a major urban settlement on the periphery of the [Maya civilization](/wiki/Mayan_civilization "Mayan civilization") during the Preclassic Period and was heavily involved in the trading of valuable items like ceramics, obsidian, cacao and hematite. The settlement was heavily damaged around 430 AD by a volcanic eruption, after which it never regained its former prominence.[[32]](#cite_note-38) Another major Pre-Columbian settlement is [Cara Sucia](/wiki/Cara_Sucia_(Mesoamerican_site) "Cara Sucia (Mesoamerican site)") in the far west of the country, which began as a small settlement around 800 BC at the beginning of the Middle Preclassic, during the [Late Classic](/wiki/Late_Classic "Late Classic") (600–900 AD), Cara Sucia emerged as a major urban settlement, before being abruptly destroyed during the 10th century.[[33]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Temazcal_en_Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n.jpg/250px-Temazcal_en_Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temazcal_en_Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n.jpg)

A [temazcal](/wiki/Temazcal "Temazcal") in [Joya de Cerén](/wiki/Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n "Joya de Cerén")

The [Pipil people](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people"), [Nahua](/wiki/Nahuan_languages "Nahuan languages") speaking groups migrated from [Anahuac](/wiki/Anahuac_(Aztec) "Anahuac (Aztec)") beginning around 800 AD and occupied the central and western regions of El Salvador.[[34]](#cite_note-Campbell1985-40) The Nahua Pipil were the last indigenous people to arrive in El Salvador.[[35]](#cite_note-Fowler1991-41) They called their territory *Kuskatan*, a [Nawat](/wiki/Nawat_language "Nawat language") word[[36]](#cite_note-Cárdenas1950-42) meaning "The Place of Precious Jewels", [back-formed](/wiki/Back-formation "Back-formation") into [Classical Nahuatl](/wiki/Classical_Nahuatl "Classical Nahuatl") *Cōzcatlān*, and as *Cuzcatlán*.[[37]](#cite_note-Baratta1951-43)[[38]](#cite_note-Cárdenas1964-44) It was the largest domain in Salvadoran territory up until European contact. The term *Cuzcatleco* is commonly used to identify someone of Salvadoran heritage, although the majority of the eastern population has an indigenous heritage of Lenca origin, as do their place names such as [Intipuca](/wiki/Intipuc%C3%A1 "Intipucá"), [Chirilagua](/wiki/Chirilagua "Chirilagua"), and [Lolotique](/wiki/Lolotique "Lolotique").

Most of the archaeological sites in western El Salvador such as [Lago de Guija](/wiki/Lake_G%C3%BCija "Lake Güija") and [Joya De Ceren](/wiki/Joya_de_Cer%C3%A9n "Joya de Cerén") indicate a pre-Columbian Mayan culture. [Cihuatan](/wiki/Cihuat%C3%A1n "Cihuatán") shows signs of material trade with northern Nahua culture, eastern Mayan and Lenca culture, and southern Nicaraguan and Costa Rican indigenous culture.[[39]](#cite_note-45) [Tazumal](/wiki/Tazumal "Tazumal")'s smaller B1-2 structure shows a [talud](/wiki/Talud-tablero "Talud-tablero")-[tablero](/wiki/Talud-tablero "Talud-tablero") style of architecture that is associated with Nahua culture and corresponds with their migration history from Anahuac. In eastern El Salvador, the Lenca site of [Quelepa](/wiki/Quelepa "Quelepa") is highlighted as a major pre-Columbian cultural centre and demonstrates links to the Mayan site of [Copan](/wiki/Cop%C3%A1n "Copán") in western Honduras as well as the previously mentioned sites in Chalchuapa, and Cara Sucia in western El Salvador. An investigation of the site of La Laguna in [Usulutan](/wiki/Usulut%C3%A1n "Usulután") has also produced Copador items that link it to the Lenca-Maya trade route.

### European arrival (1522)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: European arrival (1522)")]

By 1521, the indigenous population of the Mesoamerican area had been drastically reduced by the [smallpox epidemic](/wiki/History_of_smallpox "History of smallpox") that was spreading throughout the territory, although it had not yet reached pandemic levels in Cuzcatlán or the northern portion Managuara.[[40]](#cite_note-Peters2005-46)[[41]](#cite_note-Card2007-47)[[42]](#cite_note-48) The first known visit by Spaniards to what is now Salvadoran territory was made by the admiral [Andrés Niño](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Ni%C3%B1o "Andrés Niño"), who led an expedition to Central America. He disembarked in the [Gulf of Fonseca](/wiki/Gulf_of_Fonseca "Gulf of Fonseca") on 31 May 1522, at [Meanguera island](/wiki/Meanguera_del_Golfo "Meanguera del Golfo"), naming it Petronila,[[43]](#cite_note-Valdés2006-49) and then traversed to [Jiquilisco Bay](/wiki/Jiquilisco_Bay "Jiquilisco Bay") on the mouth of [Lempa River](/wiki/Lempa_River "Lempa River"). The first indigenous people to have contact with the Spanish were the Lenca of eastern El Salvador.

#### Conquest of Cuzcatlán and Managuara

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conquest of Cuzcatlán and Managuara")]

Main article: [Spanish conquest of El Salvador](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_El_Salvador "Spanish conquest of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Pedro_de_Alvarado_%28Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano%29.jpg/250px-Pedro_de_Alvarado_%28Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_de_Alvarado_(Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano).jpg)

Spanish Conquistador [Pedro de Alvarado](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alvarado "Pedro de Alvarado")

In 1524, after participating in the [conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire"), [Pedro de Alvarado](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alvarado "Pedro de Alvarado"), his brother Gonzalo, and their men crossed the [Rio Paz](/wiki/Paz_River "Paz River") southwards into Cuzcatlec territory.[[44]](#cite_note-50) Upon their arrival, Spaniards were disappointed to discover that the Pipil had little gold compared to what they had found in Guatemala or Mexico. The small amount of gold that was available had to be panned so that it could be obtained. Eventually, the Spaniards recognized the richness of the land's volcanic soil. Following this discovery, the Spanish crown began granting land based on the terms of the encomienda system.[[45]](#cite_note-51)

Pedro Alvarado led the first incursion to extend their dominion to the domain of Cuzcatlan in June 1524.[[46]](#cite_note-NicholsPool2012-52) When he arrived at the borders of the kingdom, he saw that civilians had been evacuated. Cuzcatlec warriors moved to the coastal city of [Acajutla](/wiki/Acajutla "Acajutla") and waited for Alvarado and his forces. Alvarado approached, confident that the result would be similar to what occurred in Mexico and Guatemala. He thought he would easily deal with this new indigenous force since the Mexican allies on his side and the Pipil spoke a similar language.[[47]](#cite_note-Pan_AmericanUnion1934-53)

Alvarado described the Cuzcatlec soldiers as having shields decorated with colourful exotic feathers, a vest-like armour made of three inch cotton which arrows could not penetrate, and long spears.[[48]](#cite_note-54) Both armies suffered many casualties, with a wounded Alvarado retreating and losing a lot of his men, especially among the Mexican Indian auxiliaries. Once his army had regrouped, Alvarado decided to head to the Cuzcatlan capital and again faced armed Cuzcatlec. Wounded, unable to fight and hiding in the cliffs, Alvarado sent his Spanish men on their horses to approach the Cuzcatlec to see if they would fear the horses, but they did not retreat, Alvarado recalls in his letters to [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés").[[49]](#cite_note-55)

The Cuzcatlec attacked again, and on this occasion stole Spanish weaponry. Alvarado retreated and sent Mexican messengers to demand that the Cuzcatlec warriors return the stolen weapons and surrender to their opponent's king. The Cuzcatlec responded with the famous response, "If you want your weapons, come get them". As days passed, Alvarado, fearing an ambush, sent more Mexican messengers to negotiate, but these messengers never came back and were presumably executed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Tazumal_10.jpg/250px-Tazumal_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tazumal_10.jpg)

[Tazumal](/wiki/Tazumal "Tazumal"), built between AD 250–1200, a Maya site in [Santa Ana Department](/wiki/Santa_Ana_Department "Santa Ana Department")

The Spanish efforts were firmly resisted by Pipil and their Mayan-speaking neighbours. They defeated the Spaniards and what was left of their [Tlaxcalan](/wiki/Tlaxcala "Tlaxcala") allies, forcing them to withdraw to Guatemala. After being wounded, Alvarado abandoned the war and appointed his brother, [Gonzalo de Alvarado](/wiki/Gonzalo_de_Alvarado "Gonzalo de Alvarado"), to continue the task. Two subsequent expeditions (the first in 1525, followed by a smaller group in 1528) brought the Pipil under Spanish control, since the Pipil also were weakened by a regional epidemic of smallpox. In 1525, the conquest of Cuzcatlán was completed and the city of San Salvador was established. The Spanish faced much resistance from the Pipil and were not able to reach eastern El Salvador, the area of the Lencas.

In 1526, the Spanish founded the garrison town of [San Miguel](/wiki/San_Miguel,_El_Salvador "San Miguel, El Salvador") in northern Managuara—territory of the Lenca, headed by another explorer and conquistador, [Luis de Moscoso Alvarado](/wiki/Luis_de_Moscoso_Alvarado "Luis de Moscoso Alvarado"), nephew of Pedro Alvarado. Oral history holds that a Maya-Lenca crown princess, Antu Silan Ulap I, organized resistance to the conquistadors.[[50]](#cite_note-56) The commonwealth of the Lenca was alarmed by de Moscoso's invasion, and Antu Silan travelled from village to village, uniting all the Lenca towns in present-day El Salvador and Honduras against the Spaniards. Through surprise attacks and overwhelming numbers, they were able to drive the Spanish out of San Miguel and destroy the garrison.

For ten years the Lencas prevented the Spanish from building a permanent settlement. Then the Spanish returned with more soldiers, including about 2,000 forced conscripts from indigenous communities in Guatemala. They pursued the Lenca leaders further up into the mountains of [Intibucá](/wiki/Intibuc%C3%A1_Department "Intibucá Department").

Antu Silan Ulap eventually handed over control of the Lenca resistance to [Lempira](/wiki/Lempira_(Lenca_ruler) "Lempira (Lenca ruler)") (also called Empira).[[51]](#cite_note-57) Lempira was noteworthy among indigenous leaders in that he mocked the Spanish by wearing their clothes after capturing them and using their weapons captured in battle. Lempira fought in command of thousands of Lenca forces for six more years in Managuara until he was killed in battle. The remaining Lenca forces retreated into the hills. The Spanish were then able to rebuild their garrison town of San Miguel in 1537.

### Colonial period (1525–1821)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Colonial period (1525–1821)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Iglesia_Colonial_Metapan.jpg/250px-Iglesia_Colonial_Metapan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iglesia_Colonial_Metapan.jpg)

Colonial Church of San Pedro Apóstol in [Metapán](/wiki/Metap%C3%A1n "Metapán"), built between 1736 and 1743

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Proclama_de_libertad_%28indep._Centroam%C3%A9rica%29.jpg/250px-Proclama_de_libertad_%28indep._Centroam%C3%A9rica%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proclama_de_libertad_(indep._Centroam%C3%A9rica).jpg)

A painting of the First Independence Movement celebration in San Salvador. At the centre, [José Matías Delgado](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mat%C3%ADas_Delgado "José Matías Delgado").

During the colonial period, San Salvador and San Miguel were part of the Captaincy General of Guatemala, also known as the Kingdom of Guatemala ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Reino de Guatemala*), created in 1609 as an administrative division of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"). The Salvadoran territory was administered by the mayor of Sonsonate, with San Salvador being established as an *[intendencia](/wiki/Intendancy_of_San_Salvador "Intendancy of San Salvador")* in 1786.

In 1811, a combination of internal and external factors motivated Central American elites to attempt to gain independence from the Spanish Crown. The most important internal factors were the desire of local elites to control the country's affairs free of involvement from Spanish authorities, and the long-standing Creole aspiration for independence. The main external factors motivating the independence movement were the success of the [French](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and [American](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") revolutions in the 18th century, and the weakening of the Spanish Crown's military power as a result of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), with the resulting inability to control its colonies effectively.

In November 1811 Salvadoran priest [José Matías Delgado](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mat%C3%ADas_Delgado "José Matías Delgado") rang the bells of Iglesia La Merced in San Salvador, calling for insurrection and launching the [1811 Independence Movement](/wiki/1811_Independence_Movement "1811 Independence Movement"). This insurrection was suppressed, and many of its leaders were arrested and served sentences in jail. Another insurrection was launched in 1814, which was also suppressed.

### Independence (1821)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Independence (1821)")]

In 1821, in light of unrest in Guatemala, Spanish authorities capitulated and signed the [Act of Independence of Central America](/wiki/Act_of_Independence_of_Central_America "Act of Independence of Central America"), which released all of the Captaincy General of Guatemala (comprising current territories of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua and Costa Rica and the Mexican state of [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas")) from Spanish rule and declared its independence. In 1821, El Salvador joined Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras and Nicaragua in a union named the [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Vergara_detalle_firmaacta1821.JPG/250px-Vergara_detalle_firmaacta1821.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vergara_detalle_firmaacta1821.JPG)

[José Matías Delgado](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mat%C3%ADas_Delgado "José Matías Delgado") signing the [Act of Independence of Central America](/wiki/Act_of_Independence_of_Central_America "Act of Independence of Central America"), 15 September 1821

In early 1822, the authorities of the newly independent Central American provinces, meeting in Guatemala City, [voted to join](/wiki/Central_America_under_Mexican_rule "Central America under Mexican rule") the newly constituted [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") under [Agustín de Iturbide](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide"). El Salvador resisted, insisting on autonomy for the Central American countries. A Mexican military detachment marched to San Salvador and suppressed dissent, but with the fall of Iturbide on 19 March 1823, the army decamped back to Mexico.[[52]](#cite_note-58)

Shortly thereafter, the authorities of the provinces revoked the vote to join Mexico, deciding instead to form a [federal union](/wiki/Federation "Federation") of the five remaining provinces (Chiapas permanently joined Mexico at this juncture) known as the [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America"). El Salvador declared its independence from the Federal Republic of Central America on 30 January 1841.[[53]](#cite_note-59) El Salvador joined Honduras and Nicaragua in 1896 to form the [Greater Republic of Central America](/wiki/Greater_Republic_of_Central_America "Greater Republic of Central America"), which dissolved in 1898.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Woman_and_girl_in_el_salvador_making_bread.png/250px-Woman_and_girl_in_el_salvador_making_bread.png)](/wiki/File:Woman_and_girl_in_el_salvador_making_bread.png)

A woman and a girl in El Salvador making bread, 1910

After the mid-19th century, the economy was based on coffee growing. As the world market for indigo withered away, the economy prospered or suffered as the world coffee price fluctuated. The enormous profits that coffee yielded as a monoculture export served as an impetus for the concentration of land into the hands of an oligarchy of just a few families.[[54]](#cite_note-60)

Throughout the last half of the 19th century, a succession of presidents from the ranks of the Salvadoran oligarchy, nominally both conservative and liberal, generally agreed on the promotion of coffee as the predominant [cash crop](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop"), the development of infrastructure (railroads and port facilities) primarily in support of the coffee trade, the elimination of communal landholdings to facilitate further coffee production, the passage of anti-[vagrancy](/wiki/Vagrancy "Vagrancy") laws to ensure that displaced *[campesinos](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant")* and other rural residents provided sufficient labour for the coffee *fincas* (plantations), and the suppression of rural discontent. In 1912, the national guard was created as a rural police force.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Tomas_Regalado1.jpg/250px-Tomas_Regalado1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomas_Regalado1.jpg)

Gen. [Tomás Regalado](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Regalado_(Salvadoran_politician) "Tomás Regalado (Salvadoran politician)")

In 1898, General [Tomás Regalado](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Regalado_(Salvadoran_politician) "Tomás Regalado (Salvadoran politician)") gained power by force, deposing General [Rafael Antonio Gutiérrez](/wiki/Rafael_Antonio_Guti%C3%A9rrez "Rafael Antonio Gutiérrez") and ruling as president until 1903. Once in office he revived the practice of presidents designating their successors. After serving his term, he remained active in the Salvadoran Army and was killed on 11 July 1906, at El Jicaro, during a [war against Guatemala](/wiki/Totoposte_Wars "Totoposte Wars"). Until 1913 El Salvador was politically stable, with undercurrents of popular discontent. When President [Manuel Enrique Araujo](/wiki/Manuel_Enrique_Araujo "Manuel Enrique Araujo") was killed in 1913, many hypotheses were advanced for the political motive of his murder.

Manuel Enrique Araujo's administration was followed by the [Meléndez–Quiñónez dynasty](/wiki/Mel%C3%A9ndez%E2%80%93Qui%C3%B1%C3%B3nez_dynasty "Meléndez–Quiñónez dynasty") that lasted from 1913 to 1927. [Pío Romero Bosque](/wiki/P%C3%ADo_Romero_Bosque "Pío Romero Bosque"), a former minister and a trusted collaborator of the dynasty, succeeded President [Alfonso Quiñónez Molina](/wiki/Alfonso_Qui%C3%B1%C3%B3nez_Molina "Alfonso Quiñónez Molina"), and in 1930 announced free elections, in which [Arturo Araujo](/wiki/Arturo_Araujo "Arturo Araujo") came to power on 1 March 1931, in what was considered the country's first freely contested election. His government lasted only nine months before it was overthrown by junior military officers, who accused his [Salvadoran Laborist Party](/wiki/Salvadoran_Laborist_Party "Salvadoran Laborist Party") of lacking political and governmental experience and of using its government offices inefficiently. Arturo Araujo faced general popular discontent, as the people had expected economic reforms and the redistribution of land. There were demonstrations in front of the National Palace from the first week of his administration. His vice president and minister of war was General [Maximiliano Hernández Martínez](/wiki/Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Mart%C3%ADnez "Maximiliano Hernández Martínez").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/General_Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Mart%C3%ADnez%2C_President_of_El_Salvador.jpg/250px-General_Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Mart%C3%ADnez%2C_President_of_El_Salvador.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Mart%C3%ADnez,_President_of_El_Salvador.jpg)

General [Maximiliano Hernández Martínez](/wiki/Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Mart%C3%ADnez "Maximiliano Hernández Martínez"), President of El Salvador (1931–1944)

In December 1931, a [coup d'état was organized](/wiki/1931_Salvadoran_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1931 Salvadoran coup d'état") by junior officers and led by Martínez. Only the First Regiment of Cavalry and the National Police defended the presidency (the National Police had been on its payroll), but later that night, after hours of fighting, the badly outnumbered defenders surrendered to rebel forces. The Directorate, composed of officers, hid behind a shadowy figure,[[55]](#cite_note-61) a rich anti-communist banker called Rodolfo Duke, and later installed the vice-president Martínez as president. The revolt was probably caused by the army's discontent at not having been paid by President Araujo for some months. Araujo left the National Palace and unsuccessfully tried to organize forces to defeat the revolt.

The U.S. Minister in El Salvador met with the Directorate and later recognized the government of Martínez, which agreed to hold presidential elections. He resigned six months prior to running for re-election, winning back the presidency as the only candidate on the ballot. He ruled from 1935 to 1939, then from 1939 to 1943. He began a fourth term in 1944 but resigned in May after a general strike. Martínez had said he was going to respect the constitution, which stipulated he could not be re-elected, but he refused to keep his promise.

#### La Matanza

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: La Matanza")]

Main article: [La Matanza](/wiki/La_Matanza "La Matanza")

Beginning in January 1932, there was brutal suppression of a rural revolt known as [La Matanza](/wiki/La_Matanza "La Matanza"). In the unstable political climate of the previous few years, social activist and revolutionary leader [Farabundo Martí](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD "Farabundo Martí") helped found the Communist Party of Central America, and led a communist alternative to the Red Cross, called "[International Red Aid](/wiki/International_Red_Aid "International Red Aid")", serving as one of its representatives. Their goal was to help poor and underprivileged Salvadorans through the use of [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") ideology.

In December 1930, at the height of the country's economic and social depression, Martí was once again exiled because of his popularity among the nation's poor and rumours of his upcoming nomination for president the following year. Once Araujo was elected president in 1931, Martí returned to El Salvador and, along with Alfonso Luna and Mario Zapata, began the movement that was later truncated by the military.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Salvadoran_General_Salvador_Castaneda_Castro_with_his_cabinet_members_1940s.jpg/250px-Salvadoran_General_Salvador_Castaneda_Castro_with_his_cabinet_members_1940s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salvadoran_General_Salvador_Castaneda_Castro_with_his_cabinet_members_1940s.jpg)

[Salvador Castaneda Castro](/wiki/Salvador_Castaneda_Castro "Salvador Castaneda Castro") with his cabinet members 1940s.[[56]](#cite_note-Histórica_General_Martínez-62)[[57]](#cite_note-Historia_de_la_Corte_Suprema_de_Justicia_de_El_Salvador-63)

On 22 January 1932, thousands of poorly armed peasants in the western part of El Salvador revolted against
the government and Martínez. The rebellion occurred amid widespread unrest over suppression of democratic political freedoms following the cancellation of the results of the 1932 legislative election. The rebels were led by [Feliciano Ama](/wiki/Feliciano_Ama "Feliciano Ama") and [Farabundo Martí](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD "Farabundo Martí") and were largely composed of Indigenous people and communists. The rebellion made gains initially, capturing several towns and cities across the western part of the country, killing an estimated 2,000 people. The government suppressed the rebellion brutally, killing between 10,000 and 40,000 people, mostly [Pipil](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people") peasants. Many of the rebellion's leaders, including Ama and Martí, were captured and executed.[[58]](#cite_note-Ching-64)[[59]](#cite_note-Lindo-Fuentes-65)[[60]](#cite_note-Beverly-66)

Historically, the high Salvadoran population density has contributed to tensions with neighbouring [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), as land-poor Salvadorans emigrated to less densely populated Honduras and established themselves as squatters on unused or underused land. This phenomenon was a major cause of the [1969 Football War](/wiki/Football_War "Football War") between the two countries.[[61]](#cite_note-67) As many as 130,000 Salvadorans were forcibly expelled or fled from Honduras.[[62]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Jose_Napoleon_Duarte_1980-1990.jpg/250px-Jose_Napoleon_Duarte_1980-1990.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jose_Napoleon_Duarte_1980-1990.jpg)

[José Napoleón Duarte](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Napole%C3%B3n_Duarte "José Napoleón Duarte")

The [Christian Democratic Party](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Party_(El_Salvador) "Christian Democratic Party (El Salvador)") (PDC) and the [National Conciliation Party](/wiki/National_Coalition_Party_(El_Salvador) "National Coalition Party (El Salvador)") (PCN) were active in Salvadoran politics from 1960 until 2011, when they were disbanded by the Supreme Court because they had failed to win enough votes in the 2004 presidential election.[[63]](#cite_note-69) Both parties have since reconstituted. They share common ideals, but one represents the middle class and the latter the interests of the Salvadoran military.

PDC leader [José Napoleón Duarte](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Napole%C3%B3n_Duarte "José Napoleón Duarte") was the mayor of San Salvador from 1964 to 1970, winning three elections during the regime of PCN president, [Julio Adalberto Rivera Carballo](/wiki/Julio_Adalberto_Rivera_Carballo "Julio Adalberto Rivera Carballo"), who allowed free elections for mayors and the National Assembly. Duarte later ran for president with a political grouping called the [National Opposition Union](/wiki/National_Opposition_Union_(El_Salvador) "National Opposition Union (El Salvador)") (UNO) but was defeated in the 1972 presidential elections. He lost to the ex-minister of interior, Colonel [Arturo Armando Molina](/wiki/Arturo_Armando_Molina "Arturo Armando Molina"), in an election that was widely viewed as fraudulent; Molina was declared the winner even though Duarte was said to have received a majority of the votes. Duarte, at some army officers' requests, supported a revolt to protest the election fraud, but was captured, tortured and later exiled. Duarte returned to the country in 1979 to enter politics after working on projects in Venezuela as an engineer.

### Salvadoran Civil War (1979–1992)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Salvadoran Civil War (1979–1992)")]

Further information: [Salvadoran Civil War](/wiki/Salvadoran_Civil_War "Salvadoran Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/32-0032_Monumento_a_la_Memoria_y_la_Verdad.jpg/250px-32-0032_Monumento_a_la_Memoria_y_la_Verdad.jpg)](/wiki/File:32-0032_Monumento_a_la_Memoria_y_la_Verdad.jpg)

A monument carved in black marble which contains the names of thousands of victims of [massacres that occurred during the civil war](/wiki/List_of_massacres_in_El_Salvador "List of massacres in El Salvador")

[Carlos Humberto Romero](/wiki/Carlos_Humberto_Romero "Carlos Humberto Romero") was the final president of the [country's military dictatorship which began in 1931](/wiki/Military_dictatorship_in_El_Salvador "Military dictatorship in El Salvador"). The U.S. had been Romero's biggest supporter, but by October 1979, the Carter administration decided that El Salvador needed regime change.[[64]](#cite_note-70)

On 15 October 1979, a [coup d'état](/wiki/1979_Salvadoran_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1979 Salvadoran coup d'état") brought the [Revolutionary Government Junta](/wiki/Revolutionary_Government_Junta_of_El_Salvador "Revolutionary Government Junta of El Salvador") (JRG) to power. It nationalized many private companies and took over much privately owned land. The purpose of this new junta was to stop the revolutionary movement already underway in response to Duarte's stolen election. Nevertheless, the oligarchy opposed [agrarian reform](/wiki/Agrarian_reform "Agrarian reform"), and a junta formed with young reformist elements from the army such as Colonels [Adolfo Arnoldo Majano](/wiki/Adolfo_Arnoldo_Majano "Adolfo Arnoldo Majano") and [Jaime Abdul Gutiérrez](/wiki/Jaime_Abdul_Guti%C3%A9rrez "Jaime Abdul Gutiérrez"),[[65]](#cite_note-71)[[66]](#cite_note-72) as well as with progressives such as [Guillermo Ungo](/wiki/Guillermo_Ungo "Guillermo Ungo") and Alvarez.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/O.Romero_1979_autographed_photo.jpg/250px-O.Romero_1979_autographed_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:O.Romero_1979_autographed_photo.jpg)

[Archbishop Romero](/wiki/Archbishop_Romero "Archbishop Romero") spoke out against social injustice and violence amid the escalating conflict between the military government and left-wing insurgents that led to the Salvadoran Civil War.

Pressure from the oligarchy soon dissolved the junta because of its inability to control the army in its repression of the people fighting for unionization rights, agrarian reform, better wages, accessible health care and freedom of expression. In the meantime, the guerrilla movement was spreading to all sectors of Salvadoran society. Middle and high school students were organized in MERS (Movimiento Estudiantil Revolucionario de Secundaria, Revolutionary Movement of Secondary Students); college students were involved with AGEUS (Asociacion de Estudiantes Universitarios Salvadorenos; Association of Salvadoran College Students); and workers were organized in BPR (Bloque Popular Revolucionario, Popular Revolutionary Block). In October 1980, several other major guerrilla groups of the Salvadoran left had formed the [Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD_National_Liberation_Front "Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front"), or FMLN. By the end of the 1970s, government-contracted death squads were killing about 10 people each day. Meanwhile, the FMLN had 6,000 to 8,000 active guerrillas and hundreds of thousands of part-time militia, supporters, and sympathizers.[[67]](#cite_note-73)

The U.S. supported and financed the creation of a second junta to change the political environment and stop the spread of a leftist insurrection. Napoleón Duarte was recalled from his exile in Venezuela to head this new junta. However, a revolution was already underway and his new role as head of the junta was seen by the general population as opportunistic. He was unable to influence the outcome of the insurrection.

[Óscar Romero](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Romero "Óscar Romero"), the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") Archbishop of San Salvador, denounced injustices and massacres committed against civilians by government forces. He was considered "the voice of the voiceless", but he was assassinated by a [death squad](/wiki/Death_squads_in_El_Salvador "Death squads in El Salvador") while saying Mass on 24 March 1980.[[68]](#cite_note-74) Some consider this to be the beginning of the full [Salvadoran Civil War](/wiki/Salvadoran_Civil_War "Salvadoran Civil War"), which lasted from 1980 to 1992.

An unknown number of people "disappeared" during the conflict, and the UN reports that more than 75,000 were killed.[[69]](#cite_note-75) The [Salvadoran Army](/wiki/Salvadoran_Army "Salvadoran Army")'s US-trained [Atlácatl Battalion](/wiki/Atl%C3%A1catl_Battalion "Atlácatl Battalion") was responsible for the [El Mozote massacre](/wiki/El_Mozote_massacre "El Mozote massacre") where more than 800 civilians were murdered, over half of them children, the [El Calabozo massacre](/wiki/El_Calabozo_massacre "El Calabozo massacre"), and the [murder of UCA scholars](/wiki/Murder_of_UCA_scholars "Murder of UCA scholars").[[70]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Protest_against_the_Salvadoran_Civil_War_Chicago_1989_5.jpg/250px-Protest_against_the_Salvadoran_Civil_War_Chicago_1989_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protest_against_the_Salvadoran_Civil_War_Chicago_1989_5.jpg)

Protest against US involvement in the Salvadoran Civil War in Chicago, Illinois, in March 1989

On 16 January 1992, the government of El Salvador, represented by president [Alfredo Cristiani](/wiki/Alfredo_Cristiani "Alfredo Cristiani"), and the FMLN, represented by the commanders of the five guerrilla groups – [Schafik Hándal](/wiki/Schafik_H%C3%A1ndal "Schafik Hándal"), [Joaquín Villalobos](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Villalobos "Joaquín Villalobos"), [Salvador Sánchez Cerén](/wiki/Salvador_S%C3%A1nchez_Cer%C3%A9n "Salvador Sánchez Cerén"), Francisco Jovel and [Eduardo Sancho](/wiki/Ferm%C3%A1n_Cienfuegos "Fermán Cienfuegos"), all signed peace agreements brokered by the United Nations ending the 12-year civil war. This event, held at [Chapultepec Castle](/wiki/Chapultepec_Castle "Chapultepec Castle") in Mexico, was attended by U.N. dignitaries and other representatives of the international community. After signing the armistice, the president stood and shook hands with the newly ex-guerrilla commanders, an action which was widely admired.

### Post-war (1992–2019)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Post-war (1992–2019)")]

The [Chapultepec Peace Accords](/wiki/Chapultepec_Peace_Accords "Chapultepec Peace Accords") mandated reductions in the size of the army, and the dissolution of the National Police, the Treasury Police, the National Guard and the Civilian Defence, a paramilitary group. A new Civil Police was to be organized. Judicial immunity for crimes committed by the armed forces ended; the government agreed to submit to the recommendations of a [Commission on the Truth for El Salvador](/wiki/Commission_on_the_Truth_for_El_Salvador "Commission on the Truth for El Salvador") *(Comisión de la Verdad Para El Salvador)*, which would "investigate serious acts of violence occurring since 1980, and the nature and effects of the violence, and...recommend methods of promoting national reconciliation". In 1993 the Commission delivered its findings reporting human rights violations on both sides of the conflict.[[71]](#cite_note-77) Five days later the Salvadoran legislature passed an amnesty law for all acts of violence during the period.

From 1989 until 2004, Salvadorans favoured the [Nationalist Republican Alliance](/wiki/Nationalist_Republican_Alliance "Nationalist Republican Alliance") (ARENA), voting in ARENA presidents in every election ([Alfredo Cristiani](/wiki/Alfredo_Cristiani "Alfredo Cristiani"), [Armando Calderón Sol](/wiki/Armando_Calder%C3%B3n_Sol "Armando Calderón Sol"), [Francisco Flores Pérez](/wiki/Francisco_Flores_P%C3%A9rez "Francisco Flores Pérez"), [Antonio Saca](/wiki/Antonio_Saca "Antonio Saca")) until 2009. The unsuccessful attempts of the left-wing party to win presidential elections led to its selection of a journalist rather than a former guerrilla leader as a candidate. On 15 March 2009, [Mauricio Funes](/wiki/Mauricio_Funes "Mauricio Funes"), a television figure, became the first president from the FMLN. He was inaugurated on 1 June 2009.[[72]](#cite_note-78) One focus of the Funes government has been revealing the alleged corruption from the past government.[[73]](#cite_note-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/XXIV_Conmemoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Acuerdos_de_Paz._%2823793774503%29.jpg/250px-XXIV_Conmemoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Acuerdos_de_Paz._%2823793774503%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:XXIV_Conmemoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Acuerdos_de_Paz._(23793774503).jpg)

The signatories of the Peace Agreements. on its XXIV anniversary; The agreements led to a series of transformations that marked a before and after in national history.

ARENA formally expelled Saca from the party in December 2009. With 12 loyalists in the National Assembly, Saca established his own party, the [Grand Alliance for National Unity](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_for_National_Unity "Grand Alliance for National Unity") (GANA), and entered into a tactical legislative alliance with the FMLN.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After three years in office, with Saca's GANA party providing the FMLN with a legislative majority, Funes had not taken action to either investigate or to bring corrupt former officials to justice.

Economic reforms since the early 1990s brought major benefits in terms of improved social conditions, diversification of the export sector, and access to international financial markets at investment grade level. Crime remains a major problem for the investment climate. Early in the new millennium, El Salvador's government created the Ministerio de Medio Ambiente y Recursos Naturales — the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources (MARN) — in response to climate change concerns.[[74]](#cite_note-cdkn.org-80) El Salvador adopted the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") as its currency on 1 January 2001.

In March 2014, former FMLN guerrilla leader Cerén narrowly won the [election](/wiki/2014_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2014 Salvadoran presidential election"). He was sworn in as president on 31 May 2014. He was the first former guerrilla to become the president of El Salvador.[[75]](#cite_note-bbcnews-81)

In October 2017, an El Salvador court ruled that former president Funes and one of his sons had illegally enriched themselves. Funes had sought asylum in Nicaragua in 2016.[[76]](#cite_note-82)

In September 2018, former president Saca was sentenced to 10 years in prison after he pleaded guilty to diverting more than US$300 million in state funds to his own businesses and third parties.[[77]](#cite_note-83)

### 2019–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2019–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Nayib_Bukele_talks_at_his_inauguration_ceremony.jpg/250px-Nayib_Bukele_talks_at_his_inauguration_ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nayib_Bukele_talks_at_his_inauguration_ceremony.jpg)

Nayib Bukele speaks at his inauguration ceremony.

On 1 June 2019, [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele") became the new president of El Salvador.[[78]](#cite_note-84) Bukele was the winner of February [2019 presidential election](/wiki/2019_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2019 Salvadoran presidential election"). He represented GANA, as he was denied participating with the newly formed Nuevas Ideas party. ARENA and the FMLN, El Salvador's two main parties, had dominated politics in El Salvador over the past three decades.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

According to a report by the [International Crisis Group](/wiki/International_Crisis_Group "International Crisis Group") (ICG) 2020, the homicide rate in El Salvador had dropped by as much as 60% since Bukele became president in June 2019. Although the government officially denied any collusion with organised crime, press reports based on testimony from government members and security officials stated a "non-aggression deal" between parts of the government and the gangs existed.[[80]](#cite_note-86)

The party [Nuevas Ideas](/wiki/Nuevas_Ideas "Nuevas Ideas") (NI, "New Ideas"), founded by Bukele, with its ally (GANA) won around 63% of the vote in the February 2021 [legislative elections](/wiki/2021_Salvadoran_legislative_election "2021 Salvadoran legislative election"). His party and allies won 61 seats, well over the coveted supermajority of 56 seats in the 84-seat parliament, allowing for uncontested decisions at the legislative level. The supermajority permits President Bukele's party to appoint judiciary members and pass laws with little to no opposition, for instance, to remove presidential term limits.[[81]](#cite_note-87)[[82]](#cite_note-centralamerica-88)

On 8 June 2021, at the initiative of President Bukele, pro-government deputies in the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador") voted [legislation](/wiki/Bitcoin_Law "Bitcoin Law") to make [bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") [legal tender](/wiki/Legal_tender "Legal tender") in El Salvador.[[83]](#cite_note-btcasamblea202106-89)[[84]](#cite_note-btcelsalvador20210608-90) In September 2021, El Salvador's Supreme Court ruled to allow Bukele to run for a second term in 2024, despite the fact that the constitution prohibits the president to serve two consecutive terms in office. The decision was organized by judges appointed to the court by Bukele.[[85]](#cite_note-91) On 25 February 2021, El Salvador became the first Central American country to be awarded certification for the elimination of [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") by the [WHO](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization").[[86]](#cite_note-92)

In January 2022, the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF) urged El Salvador to reverse its decision to make [cryptocurrency](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency") legal tender. Bitcoin had rapidly lost about half of its value, meaning economic difficulties and, as of May 2022, with government bonds trading at 40% of their original value, the prospect of a looming [sovereign default](/wiki/Sovereign_default "Sovereign default");[[87]](#cite_note-93) however, as of April 2025, bitcoin's value has doubled since El Salvador first designated it as legal tender. Bukele announced back in January 2022 plans to build [Bitcoin City](/wiki/Bitcoin_City "Bitcoin City") at the base of a volcano in El Salvador.[[88]](#cite_note-94)

In 2022, the Salvadoran government initiated a massive [fight against criminal gangs](/wiki/2022_Salvadoran_gang_crackdown "2022 Salvadoran gang crackdown") and gang-related violence. A state of emergency was declared on 27 March and was extended on 20 July. More than 53,000 suspected gang members were arrested, precipitating the highest reported [incarceration rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_incarceration_rate "List of countries by incarceration rate") in the world.[[89]](#cite_note-95)[[90]](#cite_note-96) The crackdown has reportedly produced hundreds of deaths of detainees, with international human rights organizations such as [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") declaring it the worst abuse of human rights in the country since its civil war.[[91]](#cite_note-97)

On 30 November 2023, the Legislative Assembly granted Bukele and Vice President Felix Ulloa a leave of absence so that they could focus on their [2024 re-election campaign](/wiki/2024_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2024 Salvadoran presidential election").[[92]](#cite_note-98) Bukele was succeeded by [Claudia Rodríguez de Guevara](/wiki/Claudia_Rodr%C3%ADguez_de_Guevara "Claudia Rodríguez de Guevara") as acting president, the first female president in Salvadoran history.[[93]](#cite_note-99)

In January 2024, it was announced that homicide rate dropped nearly 70% year over year, with 154 in 2023 compared to 495 homicides in 2022.[[94]](#cite_note-100) On 4 February 2024, Bukele won re-election with 84% of the vote in the [presidential election](/wiki/2024_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2024 Salvadoran presidential election").[[95]](#cite_note-101) His party Nuevas Ideas won 58 of the parliament's 60 seats.[[96]](#cite_note-102) On 1 June 2024, he was sworn in for his second five-year term.[[97]](#cite_note-103) In February 2025, El Salvador’s Congress agreed to remove Bitcoin's legal tender status, following pressure from the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").[[98]](#cite_note-104) In late-July 2025, the Legislative Assembly approved of changes to the [Constitution of El Salvador](/wiki/Constitution_of_El_Salvador "Constitution of El Salvador"), which would remove presidential term-limits, and extend individual terms from 5 years to 6.[[99]](#cite_note-105) The Legislative Assembly also moved the next presidential election from 2029 to [2027](/wiki/2027_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2027 Salvadoran presidential election") (making it coterminous with [the legislative elections](/wiki/2027_Salvadoran_legislative_election "2027 Salvadoran legislative election") that year), after which the constitutional changes would take effect.[[100]](#cite_note-106)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of El Salvador](/wiki/Geography_of_El_Salvador "Geography of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/El_Salvador_Topography.png/330px-El_Salvador_Topography.png)](/wiki/File:El_Salvador_Topography.png)

El Salvador's topography

El Salvador lies in the isthmus of Central America between latitudes [13°](/wiki/13th_parallel_north "13th parallel north") and [15°N](/wiki/15th_parallel_north "15th parallel north"), and longitudes [87°](/wiki/87th_meridian_west "87th meridian west") and [91°W](/wiki/91st_meridian_west "91st meridian west"). It stretches 270 km (168 mi) from west-northwest to east-southeast and 142 km (88 mi) north to south, with a total area of 21,041 km2 (8,124 sq mi). As the smallest and most densely populated country in continental [America](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), El Salvador is affectionately called *Pulgarcito de America* (the "[Tom Thumb](/wiki/Tom_Thumb "Tom Thumb") of the Americas"). El Salvador shares borders with Guatemala and Honduras, as well as a coastline with the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). The total national boundary length is 546 km (339 mi): 203 km (126 mi) with Guatemala and 343 km (213 mi) with Honduras. It is the only Central American country that has no Caribbean coastline. The coastline on the Pacific is 307 km (191 mi) long.

El Salvador has over 300 rivers, the most important of which is the [Rio Lempa](/wiki/Lempa_River "Lempa River"). Originating in Guatemala, the Rio Lempa cuts across the northern range of mountains, flows along much of the central plateau, and cuts through the southern volcanic range to empty into the Pacific. It is El Salvador's only navigable river. It and its tributaries drain about half of the country's area. Other rivers are generally short and drain the Pacific lowlands or flow from the central plateau through gaps in the southern mountain range to the Pacific. These include the [Goascorán](/wiki/Goascor%C3%A1n_River "Goascorán River"), [Jiboa](/wiki/Jiboa_River "Jiboa River"), [Torola](/wiki/Torola_River "Torola River"), [Paz](/wiki/Paz_River "Paz River") and the [Río Grande de San Miguel](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Grande_de_San_Miguel "Río Grande de San Miguel").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Santa_Ana_-_El_Salvador_%2850899359306%29.jpg/250px-Santa_Ana_-_El_Salvador_%2850899359306%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Ana_-_El_Salvador_(50899359306).jpg)

Ilamatepec (Santa Ana) Volcano

The geography of El Salvador is volcanic. El Salvador is a country located on the [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire"), where the majority of the earth's volcanos and earthquakes occur.[[101]](#cite_note-107) The most notable volcano is [Volcan Chaparrastique](/wiki/San_Miguel_(volcano) "San Miguel (volcano)") (San Miguel Volcano), which also exhibits the most volcanic activity. The tallest volcano is [Ilamatepec](/wiki/Santa_Ana_Volcano "Santa Ana Volcano")[[102]](#cite_note-108) (Santa Ana Volcano), reaching 7,821 feet (2,384 meters) above sea level. Along with these, [there are 20 other volcanoes](/wiki/List_of_volcanoes_in_El_Salvador "List of volcanoes in El Salvador"), many which are active, or potentially active. El Salvador has the second highest number of volcanoes of any Central American country.[[103]](#cite_note-109)

There are several lakes enclosed by volcanic craters in El Salvador, the most important of which are [Lake Ilopango](/wiki/Lake_Ilopango "Lake Ilopango") (70 km2 or 27 sq mi) and [Lake Coatepeque](/wiki/Lake_Coatepeque "Lake Coatepeque") (26 km2 or 10 sq mi). [Lake Güija](/wiki/Lake_G%C3%BCija "Lake Güija") is El Salvador's largest natural lake (44 km2 or 17 sq mi). Several artificial lakes were created by the damming of the Lempa, the largest of which is [Cerrón Grande Reservoir](/wiki/Cerr%C3%B3n_Grande_Reservoir "Cerrón Grande Reservoir") (135 km2 or 52 sq mi). There are a total 320 km2 (123.6 sq mi) of water within El Salvador's borders.

The highest point in El Salvador is [Cerro El Pital](/wiki/Cerro_El_Pital "Cerro El Pital"), at 2,730 metres (8,957 ft), on the border with Honduras. Two parallel mountain ranges cross El Salvador to the west with a central plateau between them and a narrow coastal plain hugging the Pacific. These physical features divide the country into two physiographic regions. The mountain ranges and central plateau, covering 85% of the land, comprise the interior highlands. The remaining coastal plains are referred to as the Pacific lowlands.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of El Salvador](/wiki/Climate_of_El_Salvador "Climate of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Koppen-Geiger_Map_SLV_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_SLV_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_SLV_present.svg)

The [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of El Salvador's territory

El Salvador has a [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") climate with pronounced wet and dry seasons.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Temperatures vary primarily with elevation and show little seasonal change.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) The Pacific lowlands are uniformly hot and humid. The central plateau and mountain areas are more moderate.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110)

The rainy season, known locally as *invierno*, extends from May to October.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Almost all the annual rainfall during this time, and yearly rain totals, particularly on southern-facing mountain slopes, can be as high as 2,000 millimeters (78.7 in).[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Protected areas and the central plateau receive lesser, although still significant, amounts.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Rainfall during this season generally comes from low pressure over the Pacific and usually falls in heavy afternoon thunderstorms.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Although [hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") occasionally form in the Pacific, they seldom affect El Salvador, with the notable exception of [Hurricane Mitch](/wiki/Hurricane_Mitch "Hurricane Mitch") in 1998 (which actually formed over the Atlantic Basin) and [Hurricane Emily](/wiki/1973_Pacific_hurricane_season#Hurricane_Emily "1973 Pacific hurricane season") in 1973.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110)

From November to April, the northeast [trade winds](/wiki/Trade_winds "Trade winds") control weather patterns.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) During these months, air flowing from the Caribbean has lost most of its precipitation while passing over the mountains in Honduras.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) By the time this air reaches El Salvador, it is dry, hot, and hazy.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) This season is known locally as *verano*, or summer.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110)

Temperatures vary little with season; elevation is the primary determinant.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) The Pacific lowlands are the hottest region, with annual averages ranging from 25 to 29 °C (77.0 to 84.2 °F).[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) San Salvador is representative of the central plateau, with an annual average temperature of 23 °C (73 °F) and absolute high and low readings of 38 and 6 °C (100.4 and 42.8 °F), respectively.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110) Mountain areas are the coolest, with annual averages from 12 to 23 °C (53.6 to 73.4 °F) and minimum temperatures sometimes approaching freezing.[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110)

### Natural disasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Natural disasters")]

#### Extreme weather events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Extreme weather events")]

El Salvador's position on the Pacific Ocean makes it subject to severe weather conditions, including heavy rainstorms and severe droughts, both of which may be made more extreme by the [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") and [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña") effects.[[105]](#cite_note-111) Hurricanes occasionally form in the Pacific, with the notable exception of [Hurricane Mitch](/wiki/Hurricane_Mitch "Hurricane Mitch"), which formed in the Atlantic and crossed Central America.

In the summer of 2001 a severe drought destroyed 80% of El Salvador's crops, causing famine in the countryside.[[106]](#cite_note-112)[[107]](#cite_note-113) On 4 October 2005, severe rains resulted in dangerous flooding and landslides, which caused at least 50 deaths.[[108]](#cite_note-tdfiyp-114)

#### Earthquakes and volcanic activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Earthquakes and volcanic activity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Vulkan_Chaparrastique%2C_El_Salvador_2013_01.JPG/250px-Vulkan_Chaparrastique%2C_El_Salvador_2013_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vulkan_Chaparrastique,_El_Salvador_2013_01.JPG)

[San Miguel](/wiki/San_Miguel_(volcano) "San Miguel (volcano)") volcano in 2013

El Salvador lies along the Pacific [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire") and is subject to significant tectonic activity, including frequent [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") and [volcanic](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") activity. The capital San Salvador was destroyed in 1756 and [1854](/wiki/1854_San_Salvador_earthquake "1854 San Salvador earthquake"), and it suffered heavy damage in the 1919, 1982, and 1986 tremors. Recent examples include the [magnitude](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale") 7.7 [earthquake on 13 January 2001](/wiki/January_2001_El_Salvador_earthquake "January 2001 El Salvador earthquake") that caused a [landslide](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide") that killed more than 800 people.[[108]](#cite_note-tdfiyp-114) [Another earthquake](/wiki/February_2001_El_Salvador_earthquake "February 2001 El Salvador earthquake") a month later, on 13 February 2001, killed 255 people and damaged about 20% of the country's housing. A [5.7 Mw](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale") earthquake in 1986 resulted in 1,500 deaths, 10,000 injuries, and 100,000 people left homeless.[[109]](#cite_note-115)[[110]](#cite_note-116)

El Salvador has over twenty volcanoes. Two of them, San Miguel and [Izalco](/wiki/Izalco_(volcano) "Izalco (volcano)"), have been active in recent years. From the early 19th century to the mid-1950s, Izalco erupted with a regularity that earned it the name "Lighthouse of the Pacific". Its brilliant flares were clearly visible for great distances at sea, and at night its glowing lava turned it into a brilliant luminous cone. The most recent destructive volcanic eruption took place on 1 October 2005, when the [Santa Ana Volcano](/wiki/Santa_Ana_Volcano "Santa Ana Volcano") spewed a cloud of ash, hot mud and rocks that fell on nearby villages and caused two deaths. The most severe volcanic eruption in this area occurred in the 5th century AD, when the [Ilopango](/wiki/Lake_Ilopango "Lake Ilopango") volcano erupted with a [VEI](/wiki/Volcanic_Explosivity_Index "Volcanic Explosivity Index") strength of 6, producing widespread [pyroclastic flows](/wiki/Pyroclastic_flow "Pyroclastic flow") and devastating [Mayan cities](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization").[[111]](#cite_note-117)

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Eumomota_superciliosa.jpg/250px-Eumomota_superciliosa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eumomota_superciliosa.jpg)

The [torogoz](/wiki/Torogoz "Torogoz") is El Salvador's national bird.

It is estimated that there are 500 species of birds, 1,000 species of butterflies, 400 species of orchids, 800 species of trees, and 800 species of marine saltwater fish in El Salvador.

Of the eight species of sea turtles in the world, six of them nest on the coasts of Central America, and four make their home on the Salvadoran coast: the [leatherback turtle](/wiki/Leatherback_sea_turtle "Leatherback sea turtle"), the [hawksbill](/wiki/Hawksbill_sea_turtle "Hawksbill sea turtle"), the [green sea turtle](/wiki/Green_sea_turtle "Green sea turtle"), and the [olive ridley](/wiki/Olive_ridley_sea_turtle "Olive ridley sea turtle"). The hawksbill is critically endangered.

Recent conservation efforts provide hope for the future of the country's biological diversity. In 1997, the government established the Ministry of the Environment and Natural Resources. In 1999, a general environmental framework law was approved by the National Assembly. Several non-governmental organizations are doing work to safeguard some of El Salvador's most important forested areas. Foremost among these is SalvaNatura, which manages [El Imposible](/wiki/El_Imposible_National_Park "El Imposible National Park"), El Salvador's largest national park under an agreement with El Salvador's environmental authorities.

El Salvador is home to six terrestrial ecosystems: [Central American montane forests](/wiki/Central_American_montane_forests "Central American montane forests"), [Sierra Madre de Chiapas moist forests](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_de_Chiapas_moist_forests "Sierra Madre de Chiapas moist forests"), [Central American dry forests](/wiki/Central_American_dry_forests "Central American dry forests"), [Central American pine-oak forests](/wiki/Central_American_pine-oak_forests "Central American pine-oak forests"), [Gulf of Fonseca mangroves](/wiki/Gulf_of_Fonseca_mangroves "Gulf of Fonseca mangroves"), and [Northern Dry Pacific Coast mangroves](/wiki/Northern_Dry_Pacific_Coast_mangroves "Northern Dry Pacific Coast mangroves").[[112]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-118) It had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 4.06/10, ranking it 136th globally out of 172 countries.[[113]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-119)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Presidente_Nayib_Bukele_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Presidente_Nayib_Bukele_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presidente_Nayib_Bukele_(cropped).jpg)

[Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele")  
[President of El Salvador](/wiki/President_of_El_Salvador "President of El Salvador")  
since 2019

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/F%C3%A9lix_Ulloa_%28MEL5323%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-F%C3%A9lix_Ulloa_%28MEL5323%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A9lix_Ulloa_(MEL5323)_(cropped).jpg)

[Félix Ulloa](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Ulloa "Félix Ulloa")  
[Vice President of El Salvador](/wiki/Vice_President_of_El_Salvador "Vice President of El Salvador")  
since 2019

The 1983 constitution has the highest legal authority in the country. El Salvador has a democratic and representative government, whose three bodies are:

1. The Executive Branch, headed by the President of the Republic, who is elected by direct vote and remains in office for five years with no re-election but can be elected after sitting out one electoral period. The president has a Cabinet of Ministers whom he appoints, and is also the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces.
2. The Legislative Branch, called El Salvador's Legislative Assembly (unicameral), consisting of 84 deputies.
3. The Judiciary, headed by the Supreme Court, which is composed of 15 judges, one of them being elected as President of the Judiciary.

The political framework of El Salvador is a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") with a multiform, multi-party system. The [president](/wiki/President_of_El_Salvador "President of El Salvador"), currently [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele"), is both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") is exercised by the government. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") is vested in both the government and the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador"). The country also has an independent judiciary and [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_El_Salvador "Supreme Court of El Salvador"). It was ranked the 5th least electoral [democratic country in Latin America and the Caribbean](/wiki/Democracy_in_Latin_America_and_the_Caribbean "Democracy in Latin America and the Caribbean") in 2023 by [V-Dem Democracy Report](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute#Democracy_Report "V-Dem Institute").[[114]](#cite_note-vdem_report-120)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main article: [Politics of El Salvador](/wiki/Politics_of_El_Salvador "Politics of El Salvador")

Further information: [List of political parties in El Salvador](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_El_Salvador "List of political parties in El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/XIII_Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador.jpg/250px-XIII_Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador.jpg)](/wiki/File:XIII_Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador.jpg)

The [Legislative Assembly of El Salvador](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador")

El Salvador has a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). Two [political parties](/wiki/Political_parties "Political parties"), the [Nationalist Republican Alliance](/wiki/Nationalist_Republican_Alliance "Nationalist Republican Alliance") (ARENA) and the [Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD_National_Liberation_Front "Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front") (FMLN) have tended to dominate elections. ARENA candidates won four consecutive presidential elections until the election of [Mauricio Funes](/wiki/Mauricio_Funes "Mauricio Funes") of the FMLN in March 2009. The FMLN Party is leftist in ideology, and is split between the dominant [Marxist-Leninist](/wiki/Marxist-Leninist "Marxist-Leninist") faction in the legislature, and the [social liberal](/wiki/Social_liberalism "Social liberalism") wing led by Mauricio Funes until 2014.[[115]](#cite_note-edition.cnn.com-121)

The two-party dominance was broken after [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele"), a candidate from [GANA](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_for_National_Unity "Grand Alliance for National Unity") won the [2019 Salvadoran presidential election](/wiki/2019_Salvadoran_presidential_election "2019 Salvadoran presidential election").[[115]](#cite_note-edition.cnn.com-121) In February 2021, the results of [legislative election](/wiki/2021_Salvadoran_legislative_election "2021 Salvadoran legislative election") caused a major change in the politics of El Salvador. The new allied party of president Nayib Bukele, [Nuevas Ideas](/wiki/Nuevas_Ideas "Nuevas Ideas") (New Ideas) won the biggest congressional majority in the country's history.[[82]](#cite_note-centralamerica-88)

[The departments](/wiki/Departments_of_El_Salvador "Departments of El Salvador") of the Central region, especially the capital and the coastal regions, known as **departamentos rojos** (red departments) are relatively leftist. The **departamentos azules** (blue departments) in the east, western and highland regions are relatively conservative.[[75]](#cite_note-bbcnews-81)
