### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of El Salvador](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_El_Salvador "Foreign relations of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Secretary_Pompeo_Participates_in_Signing_Ceremony_for_the_CSL_Lease_Extension_-_48342132177.jpg/250px-Secretary_Pompeo_Participates_in_Signing_Ceremony_for_the_CSL_Lease_Extension_-_48342132177.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Pompeo_Participates_in_Signing_Ceremony_for_the_CSL_Lease_Extension_-_48342132177.jpg)

U.S. Secretary of State [Mike Pompeo](/wiki/Mike_Pompeo "Mike Pompeo") participates in a signing ceremony for the CSL Lease Extension with Salvadoran President [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele").

El Salvador is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and several of its specialized agencies. It is also member of the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States"), the [Central American Parliament](/wiki/Central_American_Parliament "Central American Parliament"), and the [Central American Integration System](/wiki/Central_American_Integration_System "Central American Integration System") among others. It actively participates in the Central American Security Commission, which seeks to promote regional arms control. El Salvador is a member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") and is pursuing regional free trade agreements. An active participant in the [Summit of the Americas](/wiki/Summits_of_the_Americas "Summits of the Americas") process, El Salvador chairs a working group on market access under the [Free Trade Area of the Americas](/wiki/Free_Trade_Area_of_the_Americas "Free Trade Area of the Americas") initiative.

In November 1950, El Salvador was the only country to help the newly empowered [14th Dalai Lama](/wiki/14th_Dalai_Lama "14th Dalai Lama") by supporting his [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibet_(1912%E2%80%931951) "Tibet (1912–1951)") Government cabinet minister's telegram requesting an appeal before the [General Assembly of the United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") to stop the [annexation of Tibet by the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Annexation_of_Tibet_by_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Annexation of Tibet by the People's Republic of China").[[116]](#cite_note-The_Story_of_Tibet-122) With no other countries in support, "the UN unanimously dropped the Tibetan plea from its agenda".[[104]](#cite_note-:0-110)

El Salvador is a party to the [Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court](/wiki/Rome_Statute_of_the_International_Criminal_Court "Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court").[[117]](#cite_note-123)[[118]](#cite_note-124)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Armed Forces of El Salvador](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_El_Salvador "Armed Forces of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Nayib_Bukele_on_Salvadoran_independence_day_in_2024_%28WhatsApp_Image_2024-09-15_at_5.32.30_PM_%281%29%29.jpg/250px-Nayib_Bukele_on_Salvadoran_independence_day_in_2024_%28WhatsApp_Image_2024-09-15_at_5.32.30_PM_%281%29%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nayib_Bukele_on_Salvadoran_independence_day_in_2024_(WhatsApp_Image_2024-09-15_at_5.32.30_PM_(1)).jpg)

Soldiers in 2024

The [Armed Forces of El Salvador](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_El_Salvador "Armed Forces of El Salvador") have three branches: the [Salvadoran Army](/wiki/Salvadoran_Army "Salvadoran Army"), the [Salvadoran Air Force](/wiki/Salvadoran_Air_Force "Salvadoran Air Force") and the [Navy of El Salvador](/wiki/Navy_of_El_Salvador "Navy of El Salvador"). There are around 25,000 personnel in the armed forces in total.[[119]](#cite_note-IISSp413-125)

### Incarceration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Incarceration")]

As of February 2025, El Salvador had the highest prisoner rate worldwide, with over 1,600 prisoners per 100,000 of the national population.[[120]](#cite_note-126)

El Salvador’s [Terrorism Confinement Center](/wiki/Terrorism_Confinement_Center "Terrorism Confinement Center") (CECOT) mega-prison is considered the largest prison in the Americas with a capacity of 40,000 inmates. The incarcerations have been part of president Bukele’s efforts to reduce high crime rates and gang violence.[[121]](#cite_note-127) In March 2025, the United States transferred more than 200 immigrants, alleging them to be members of a Venezuelan gang, to be imprisoned in El Salvador in violation of court orders.[[122]](#cite_note-128)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Human rights")]

Main article: [Human rights in El Salvador](/wiki/Human_rights_in_El_Salvador "Human rights in El Salvador")

[Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") has drawn attention to several arrests of police officers for unlawful police killings. Other issues to gain Amnesty International's attention include missing children, failure of law enforcement to properly investigate and prosecute crimes against women, and rendering [organized labour](/wiki/Labor_unions "Labor unions") illegal.[[123]](#cite_note-129)

Abortion is banned, with no exceptions for rape, incest, or threat to the mother's life; as a result, 180 women have been imprisoned in the last two decades, some for up to 30 years.[[124]](#cite_note-130) Discrimination against [LGBT people](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_El_Salvador "LGBT rights in El Salvador") in El Salvador is very widespread.[[125]](#cite_note-131)[[126]](#cite_note-132) In a 2013 survey by [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 53% of Salvadorans believe that [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") should not be accepted by society.[[127]](#cite_note-pewglobal.org-133) Although homosexuality itself is legal, gay marriage is legally not recognized, as proposals were rejected twice in 2006, and once again in 2009.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of El Salvador](/wiki/Economy_of_El_Salvador "Economy of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador%2C_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador%2C_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador,_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg)

GDP per capita in El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras, 1920 to 2018

El Salvador's economy has been hampered at times by natural disasters such as earthquakes and hurricanes, by government policies that mandate large economic subsidies, and by official corruption. Subsidies became such a problem that in April 2012, the International Monetary Fund suspended a $750 million loan to the central government. President Funes' chief of cabinet, Alex Segovia, acknowledged that the economy was at the "point of collapse".[[128]](#cite_note-134)

Gross domestic product ([GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product")) in [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") estimate for 2021 is US$57.95 billion growing real GDP at 4.2% for 2021.[[129]](#cite_note-IMFWEOSV-135) The service sector is the largest component of GDP at 64.1%, followed by the industrial sector at 24.7% (2008 est.) and agriculture represents 11.2% of GDP (2010 est.). The GDP grew after 1996 at an annual rate that averaged 3.2% real growth. The government committed to free market initiatives and the 2007 GDP's real growth rate hit 4.7%.[[130]](#cite_note-136) As of December 2017, net [international reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves") stood at $3.57 billion.[[131]](#cite_note-137)

It has long been a challenge in El Salvador to develop new growth sectors for a more diversified economy. In the past, the country produced gold and silver,[[132]](#cite_note-138) but recent attempts to reopen the mining sector, which were expected to add hundreds of millions of dollars to the local economy, collapsed after President Saca shut down the operations of [Pacific Rim Mining Corporation](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_Mining_Corporation "Pacific Rim Mining Corporation"). Nevertheless, according to the Central American Institute for Fiscal Studies (Instituto Centroamericano for Estudios Fiscales), the contribution of metallic mining was a minuscule 0.3% of the country's GDP between 2010 and 2015.[[133]](#cite_note-139) Saca's decision although not lacking political motives, had strong support from local residents and grassroots movements in the country. President Funes later rejected a company's application for a further permit based on the risk of cyanide contamination on one of the country's main rivers.[[134]](#cite_note-140)

As with other former colonies, El Salvador was considered a mono-export economy (an economy that depended heavily on one type of export) for many years. During colonial times, El Salvador was a thriving exporter of [indigo](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye"), but after the invention of synthetic dyes in the 19th century, the newly created modern state turned to [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") as the main export.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/San_Miguel_El_Salvador.png/250px-San_Miguel_El_Salvador.png)](/wiki/File:San_Miguel_El_Salvador.png)

[San Miguel](/wiki/San_Miguel,_El_Salvador "San Miguel, El Salvador") is an important economic centre of El Salvador and home to the "Carnival of San Miguel", one of the biggest festivals of entertainment and food in Central America.[[135]](#cite_note-Herrera-Sobek2012-141)

The government has sought to improve the collection of its current revenues, with a focus on indirect taxes. A 10% [value-added tax](/wiki/Value-added_tax "Value-added tax") (IVA in Spanish), implemented in September 1992, was raised to 13% in July 1995. Inflation has been steady and among the lowest in the region. As a result of the free trade agreements, from 2000 to 2006, total exports have grown 19% from $2.94 billion to $3.51 billion, and total imports have risen 54% from $4.95 billion to $7.63 billion. This has resulted in a 102% increase in the trade deficit, from $2.01 billion to $4.12 billion.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

In 2006, El Salvador was the first country to ratify the Central America-Dominican Republic Free Trade Agreement ([CAFTA](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Central_America_Free_Trade_Agreement "Dominican Republic–Central America Free Trade Agreement")) — negotiated by the five countries of Central America and the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic") — with the United States. CAFTA requires that the Salvadoran government adopt policies that foster [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"). CAFTA has bolstered exports of processed foods, sugar, and ethanol, and supported investment in the apparel sector, which faced Asian competition with the expiration of the [Multi Fibre Arrangement](/wiki/Multi_Fibre_Arrangement "Multi Fibre Arrangement") in 2005. In anticipation of the declines in the apparel sector's competitiveness, the previous administration sought to diversify the economy by promoting the country as a regional distribution and logistics hub, and by promoting tourism investment through tax incentives.

In June 2021, President [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele") said he would introduce legislation to make [Bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") [legal tender](/wiki/Legal_tender "Legal tender") in El Salvador.[[137]](#cite_note-cnbc20210605-143) The [Bitcoin Law](/wiki/Bitcoin_Law "Bitcoin Law") was passed by the [Legislative Assembly of El Salvador](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador") on 9 June 2021. Bitcoin officially became a legal tender on 7 September 2021.[[138]](#cite_note-144)[[11]](#cite_note-bbc.com-13)[[139]](#cite_note-ft-145) As part of the law, foreigners can gain permanent residence in El Salvador if they invest 3 Bitcoin into the country.[[140]](#cite_note-146) The implementation of the law has been met with protests,[[141]](#cite_note-147) with the majority of the country being against using Bitcoin as legal tender.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

According to a survey conducted by the Salvadoran Chamber of Commerce, as of March 2022 only 14% of merchants in the country processed at least one Bitcoin transaction.[[143]](#cite_note-Linea_2022-149) Since May 2024, an official government entity named the Bitcoin Office of El Salvador reports that the government holds 5,750 bitcoin (approximately $354 million circa May 2024) -- with nearly 474 bitcoin (approx $29 million circa May 2024) mined since September 2021 using geothermal energy from the Tecapa volcano.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

El Salvador leads the region in [remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") per capita, with inflows equivalent to nearly all export income; in 2019 2.35 million Salvadorans lived in the U.S.[[145]](#cite_note-151) and about a third of all households received remittances. Remittances from Salvadorans living in the United States, sent to family members in El Salvador, are a major source of foreign income and offset the trade deficit. Remittances have increased steadily since the early 2000s, growing from $3.32 billion, or approximately 16.2% of GDP in 2006,[[146]](#cite_note-152) to nearly $6 billion (around 20% of GDP in 2019, one of the highest rates in the world, according to the World Bank.)[[147]](#cite_note-153)

In December 2024, Nayib Bukele's [government](/wiki/Government "Government") purchased 11 new BTC for over a million dollars, thus strengthening its strategic reserves.[[148]](#cite_note-154)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Energy")]

El Salvador's [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") industry is diversified across, relying on [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"), [hydro](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"), other [renewables](/wiki/Renewable_Energy "Renewable Energy") (mainly [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_Energy "Geothermal Energy")) for local electricity production, along with a reliance on imports for [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"). El Salvador has an installed capacity of 1,983 [MW](/wiki/Watt#Megawatt "Watt") generating 5,830 [GWh](/wiki/Watt#Gigawatt "Watt") of [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") per year, 84% of this comes from renewable sources including 26.85% from geothermal (produced from the country's many [volcanoes](/wiki/Geography_of_El_Salvador#Physical_features "Geography of El Salvador")), 29.92% from hydro and the rest is from fossil fuels.[[149]](#cite_note-155)[[150]](#cite_note-156)

According to the National Energy Commission, 94.4% of total injections during January 2021 came from hydroelectric plants (28.5% - 124.43 GWh), geothermal (27.3% - 119.07 GWh), biomass (24.4% 106.43 GWh), photovoltaic solar (10.6% - 46.44 GWh) and wind (3.6% - 15.67 GWh).[[151]](#cite_note-157)[[152]](#cite_note-158)

### Telecommunications and media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Telecommunications and media")]

Main article: [Telecommunications in El Salvador](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_El_Salvador "Telecommunications in El Salvador")

El Salvador has 900,000 fixed telephone lines, 500,000 fixed broadband lines and 9.4 million mobile cellular subscriptions.[[153]](#cite_note-cia.gov-159) Much of the population is able to access the [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") through their smartphones and mobile networks, which liberal government regulation promotes mobile penetration over fixed line including the deployment of [5G](/wiki/5G "5G") coverage (which testing of began in 2020).[[153]](#cite_note-cia.gov-159) Transition to digital transmission of TV/radio networks was done in 2018 with the adaptation of the [ISDB-T](/wiki/ISDB-T_International "ISDB-T International") standard. There are hundreds of privately owned national TV networks, cable TV networks (that also carry international channels), and radio stations available; while there is also 1 government owned broadcast station.[[153]](#cite_note-cia.gov-159)

### Official corruption and foreign investment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Official corruption and foreign investment")]

Further information: [Corruption in El Salvador](/wiki/Corruption_in_El_Salvador "Corruption in El Salvador")

In an analysis of ARENA's electoral defeat in 2009, the U.S. Embassy in San Salvador pointed to [official corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_El_Salvador "Corruption in El Salvador") under the Saca administration as a significant reason for public rejection of continued ARENA government.[[154]](#cite_note-160) Subsequent policies under Funes administrations improved El Salvador to foreign investment, and the World Bank in 2014 rated El Salvador 109, a little better than Belize (118) and Nicaragua (119) in the World Bank's annual "[Ease of doing business](/wiki/Ease_of_doing_business_index "Ease of doing business index")" index.[[155]](#cite_note-161)

As per Santander Trade, a Spanish think tank in foreign investment, "Foreign investment into El Salvador has been steadily growing during the last few years. In 2013, the influx of FDI increased. Nevertheless, El Salvador receives less FDI than other countries of Central America. The government has made little progress in terms of improving the business climate. In addition to this, the limited size of its domestic market, weak infrastructures and institutions, as well as the high level of criminality have been real obstacles to investors. However, El Salvador is the second most 'business friendly' country in Central America in terms of business taxation. It also has a young and skilled labour force and a strategic geographical position. The country's membership in the DR-CAFTA, as well as its reinforced integration to the C4 countries (producers of cotton) should lead to an increase of FDI".[[156]](#cite_note-162)

Foreign companies have lately resorted to arbitration in international trade tribunals in total disagreement with Salvadoran government policies. In 2008, El Salvador sought international arbitration against Italy's Enel Green Power, on behalf of Salvadoran state-owned electric companies for a geothermal project Enel had invested in. Four years later, Enel indicated it would seek arbitration against El Salvador, blaming the government for technical problems that prevent it from completing its investment.[[157]](#cite_note-163)

The government came to its defence claiming that Art 109 of the constitution does not allow any government (regardless of the party they belong), to privatize the resources of the national soil (in this case geothermic energy). The dispute came to an end in December 2014 when both parties came to a settlement, from which no details have been released. The small country had yielded to pressure from the Washington-based powerful [ICSID](/wiki/International_Centre_for_Settlement_of_Investment_Disputes "International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes").[[158]](#cite_note-164)

In 2009, the U.S. Embassy warned that the Salvadoran government's populist policies of mandating artificially low electricity prices were damaging private sector profitability, including the interests of American investors in the energy sector.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The U.S. Embassy noted the corruption of El Salvador's judicial system and quietly urged American businesses to include "arbitration clauses, preferably with a foreign venue", when doing business in the country.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In terms of how people perceived the levels of public corruption in 2014, El Salvador ranks 80 out of 175 countries as per the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index").[[159]](#cite_note-165) El Salvador's rating compares relatively well with Panama (94 of 175) and Costa Rica (47 of 175).

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Further information: [Tourism in El Salvador](/wiki/Tourism_in_El_Salvador "Tourism in El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Palms_El_Salvador.JPG/250px-Palms_El_Salvador.JPG)](/wiki/File:Palms_El_Salvador.JPG)

Tropical beach at La Libertad

It was estimated that 1,394,000 international tourists visited El Salvador in 2014. Tourism contributed US$2970.1 million to El Salvador's GDP in 2019. This represented 11% of total GDP.[[160]](#cite_note-wttc.org-166) Tourism directly supported 80,500 jobs in 2013. This represented 3.1% of total employment in El Salvador. In 2019, tourism indirectly supported 317,200 jobs, representing 11.6% of total employment in El Salvador.[[160]](#cite_note-wttc.org-166)

Most North American and European tourists seek out El Salvador's beaches and nightlife. El Salvador's tourism landscape is slightly different from those of other Central American countries. Because of its geographic size and urbanization there are not many nature-themed tourist destinations such as ecotours or archaeological sites open to the public. Nonetheless, El Salvador remains best known for its beaches and volcanoes. The most frequently visited beaches include El Tunco, Punta Roca, El Sunzal, El Zonte beach, La Costa del Sol, El Majahual, and La Libertad beach. The most hiked volcanoes are Santa Ana and Izalco.[[161]](#cite_note-167)[[162]](#cite_note-168)[[163]](#cite_note-169)

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

The level of access [to water supply and sanitation](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_El_Salvador "Water supply and sanitation in El Salvador") has been increased significantly. A 2015 conducted study by the University of North Carolina called El Salvador the country that has achieved the greatest progress in the world in terms of increased access to water supply and sanitation and the reduction of inequity in access between urban and rural areas.[[164]](#cite_note-170) However, water resources are seriously polluted and a large part of the wastewater discharged into the environment without any treatment. Institutionally a single public institution is both de facto in charge of setting sector policy and of being the main service provider. Attempts at reforming and modernizing the sector through new laws have not borne fruit over the past 20 years.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Hospital_El_Salvador.jpg/250px-Hospital_El_Salvador.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hospital_El_Salvador.jpg)

Hospital El Salvador

In response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the government converted the country's main convention centre into [Hospital El Salvador](/wiki/Hospital_El_Salvador "Hospital El Salvador") to be the largest hospital in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). The facility was inaugurated by the president on 22 June 2020, at which time he announced the hospital conversion would be permanent because of the large investment made.[[165]](#cite_note-lanotta-171)[[166]](#cite_note-efe-172) US$25 million was spent on the first phase of the conversion of the former convention centre,[[167]](#cite_note-comercio-173) with the entire facility costing $75 million and featuring a blood bank, morgue, radiology area, among other amenities.[[166]](#cite_note-efe-172)[[167]](#cite_note-comercio-173) The hospital will have a total capacity of 1,083 ICU beds and 2,000 beds total once phase 3 is completed.[[165]](#cite_note-lanotta-171)[[168]](#cite_note-174)

The airport serving international flights in El Salvador is [Monseñor Óscar Arnulfo Romero International Airport](/wiki/Monse%C3%B1or_%C3%93scar_Arnulfo_Romero_International_Airport "Monseñor Óscar Arnulfo Romero International Airport"). This airport is located about 40 km (25 mi) southeast of San Salvador.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of El Salvador](/wiki/Demographics_of_El_Salvador "Demographics of El Salvador")

El Salvador's population was 6,314,167 in 2021,[[170]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-176)[[171]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-177) compared to 2,200,000 in 1950. In 2010 the percentage of the population below the age of 15 was 32.1%, 61% were between 15 and 65 years of age, while 6.9% were 65 years or older.[[172]](#cite_note-WPP_2012-178) The capital city of San Salvador has a population of about 2.1 million people. An estimated 42% of El Salvador's population live in rural areas. [Urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") has expanded at a phenomenal rate in El Salvador since the 1960s, with millions moving to the cities and creating associated problems for urban planning and services.

There are up to 100,000 Nicaraguans living in El Salvador.[[173]](#cite_note-179)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Around 86% of Salvadorans identify as mestizo, or mixed Amerindian and European descent, 12.7% identify as being of full European ancestry, 0.1% identify as Afro-descended while about 0.6% do not identify with any of the aforementioned categories.[[2]](#cite_note-El_Salvador_Census_2007-4)

Ethnic Groups in El Salvador

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ethnic Groups | |  | Proportion | |
| Mestizo | |  | 86.3% | |
| White | |  | 12.7% | |
| Indigenous | |  | 0.23% | |
| Black | |  | 0.13% | |
| Other | |  | 0.64% | |

Of the population, 0.23% report as fully indigenous. The ethnic groups are [Kakawira](/wiki/Cacaopera_people "Cacaopera people") which represents 0.07% of the total country's population, [Nahua](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people") (0.06%), [Lenca](/wiki/Lenca_people "Lenca people") (0.04%) and other minor groups (0.06%). Very few Amerindians have retained their customs and traditions, having over time assimilated into the dominant mestizo culture.[[176]](#cite_note-182) There is a small [Afro-Salvadoran](/wiki/Afro-Salvadorans "Afro-Salvadorans") group that is 0.13% of the total population, with Blacks, among other races, having been prevented from immigrating via government policies in the early 20th century.[[177]](#cite_note-183)[[178]](#cite_note-B-184) The descendants of enslaved Africans, however, had already integrated into the Salvadoran population and culture well before, during the colonial and post-colonial period.[[179]](#cite_note-185)

Among the various immigrant groups in El Salvador, [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") stand out.[[180]](#cite_note-186) Though few in number, their descendants have attained great economic and political power in the country, as evidenced by the election of President [Antonio Saca](/wiki/Antonio_Saca "Antonio Saca"), whose opponent in the 2004 election, [Schafik Handal](/wiki/Schafik_Handal "Schafik Handal"), was also of Palestinian descent, and the flourishing commercial, industrial, and construction firms owned by this ethnic group. The current President, [Nayib Bukele](/wiki/Nayib_Bukele "Nayib Bukele"), is also of Palestinian descent.

As of 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit), there were approximately 3.2 million Salvadorans living outside El Salvador, with the United States traditionally being the destination of choice for Salvadoran economic migrants. By 2012, there were about 2.0 million Salvadoran immigrants and [Americans of Salvadoran descent](/wiki/Salvadoran_Americans "Salvadoran Americans") in the U.S.,[[181]](#cite_note-2012AmericanCommunitySurvey-187)[[182]](#cite_note-188) making them the sixth largest immigrant group in the country.[[183]](#cite_note-189) The second destination of Salvadorans living outside is Guatemala, with more than 111,000 persons, mainly in [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City"). Salvadorans also live in other nearby countries, such as Belize, Honduras and Nicaragua.[[184]](#cite_note-190) Other countries with notable Salvadoran communities include Canada, Mexico, the United Kingdom (including the Cayman Islands), Sweden, Brazil, Italy, and Colombia.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of El Salvador](/wiki/Languages_of_El_Salvador "Languages of El Salvador")

Spanish is the official language and is spoken by virtually all inhabitants, although a very small number (around 500) of indigenous [Pipils](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people") speak [Nawat](/wiki/Pipil_language "Pipil language"). The other indigenous languages, namely [Poqomam](/wiki/Poqomam_language "Poqomam language"), [Cacaopera](/wiki/Cacaopera_language "Cacaopera language"), and [Lenca](/wiki/Salvadoran_Lenca_language "Salvadoran Lenca language"), are extinct. [Q'eqchi'](/wiki/Q%27eqchi%27_language "Q'eqchi' language") is spoken by indigenous immigrants of [Guatemalan](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") and [Belizean](/wiki/Belize "Belize") origin living in El Salvador.

The local Spanish [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") is called *[Caliche](/wiki/Salvadoran_Spanish "Salvadoran Spanish")*, which is considered informal. As in other regions of Central and South America, Salvadorans use *[voseo](/wiki/Voseo "Voseo")*. This refers to the use of "*vos*" as the second person singular pronoun, instead of "*tú*".

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in El Salvador](/wiki/Religion_in_El_Salvador "Religion in El Salvador")

The majority of the population in El Salvador is [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"). [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (43.3%) and [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (33.9%) are the two major religious groups in the country, with the Catholic Church the largest denomination.[[185]](#cite_note-IRFR2012-191) Those not affiliated with any religious group amount to 18.6% of the population.[[185]](#cite_note-IRFR2012-191) The remaining is that of 3% [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), [Hare Krishnas](/wiki/Hare_Krishnas "Hare Krishnas") sect of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_El_Salvador "Islam in El Salvador"), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists"), [Latter-day Saints](/wiki/Latter-day_Saints "Latter-day Saints"), and those adhering to indigenous religious beliefs, and 1.2% being made up of those who identify as agnostic or atheist.[[185]](#cite_note-IRFR2012-191) The number of evangelicals in the country is growing rapidly.[[186]](#cite_note-192) [Óscar Romero](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Romero "Óscar Romero"), the first Salvadoran saint, was canonized by [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") on 14 October 2018.

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Education")]

Further information: [Education in El Salvador](/wiki/Education_in_El_Salvador "Education in El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Asociaci%C3%B3n_Salvadore%C3%B1a_de_Astronom%C3%ADa_Observatorio_%22Dr._Prudencio_Llach%22.jpg/250px-Asociaci%C3%B3n_Salvadore%C3%B1a_de_Astronom%C3%ADa_Observatorio_%22Dr._Prudencio_Llach%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asociaci%C3%B3n_Salvadore%C3%B1a_de_Astronom%C3%ADa_Observatorio_%22Dr._Prudencio_Llach%22.jpg)

Dr. Prudencio Llach Observatory

The public education system in El Salvador is severely lacking in resources. Class sizes in public schools can be as large as 50 children per classroom. Salvadorans who can afford the cost often choose to send their children to private schools, which are regarded as being better-quality than public schools. Most private schools follow American, European or other advanced systems. Lower-income families are forced to rely on public education.[[187]](#cite_note-193)

Education in El Salvador is free through high school. After nine years of basic education (elementary–middle school), students have the option of a two-year high school or a three-year high school. A two-year high school prepares the student for transfer to a university. A three-year high school allows the student to graduate and enter the workforce in a vocational career, or to transfer to a university to further their education in their chosen field.[[188]](#cite_note-194)

[Universities in El Salvador](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_El_Salvador "List of universities in El Salvador") include a central public institution, the [Universidad de El Salvador](/wiki/Universidad_de_El_Salvador "Universidad de El Salvador"), and many other specialized private universities. El Salvador was ranked 98th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025, up from 108th in 2019.[[189]](#cite_note-195)[[190]](#cite_note-196)[[191]](#cite_note-197)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [Crime in El Salvador](/wiki/Crime_in_El_Salvador "Crime in El Salvador")

Since the early twenty-first century, El Salvador has experienced high crime rates, including gang-related crimes and [juvenile delinquency](/wiki/Juvenile_delinquency "Juvenile delinquency").[[192]](#cite_note-198) El Salvador had the highest murder rate in the world in 2012 but experienced a sharp decline in 2019 with a new conservative government in power.[[193]](#cite_note-199)[[194]](#cite_note-200) In 2005 it was considered an epicentre of a gang crisis, along with Guatemala and Honduras.[[195]](#cite_note-FAS-201) Several journalistic investigations indicate that the government administrations of [Carlos Mauricio Funes Cartagena](/wiki/Mauricio_Funes "Mauricio Funes")[[196]](#cite_note-202) and [Salvador Sánchez Cerén](/wiki/Salvador_S%C3%A1nchez_Cer%C3%A9n "Salvador Sánchez Cerén"),[[197]](#cite_note-203) far from working to eradicate violence and the actions of gang groups, made truces with the gangs Barrio 18 and Mara Salvatrucha[[198]](#cite_note-204) to keep a certain control over criminal activities and murders in the Salvadoran territory.

In response to this, the government has set up countless programmes to try to guide the youth away from gang membership; so far its efforts have not produced any quick results. One of the government programmes was a gang reform called "[Super Mano Dura](/wiki/La_Mano_Dura "La Mano Dura")" (Super Firm Hand). Super Mano Dura had little success and was highly criticized by the United Nations. It experienced temporary success in 2004 but there was a rise in crime after 2005.

#### Homicide rates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Homicide rates")]

In 2004, there were 41 intentional homicides per 100,000 citizens, with 60% of the homicides committed being gang-related.[[195]](#cite_note-FAS-201) In 2012, the homicide rate had increased to 66 per 100,000 inhabitants, more than triple the rate in Mexico.[[199]](#cite_note-NYRB-205)[[200]](#cite_note-206)[[201]](#cite_note-207) In 2011 there were an estimated 25,000 gang members at large in El Salvador, with another 9,000 in prison.[[199]](#cite_note-NYRB-205) The most well-known gangs, called "[maras](/wiki/Mara_(gang) "Mara (gang)")" in colloquial Spanish, are [Mara Salvatrucha](/wiki/MS-13 "MS-13") and their rivals [Barrio 18](/wiki/18th_Street_gang "18th Street gang"). Maras are hunted by [death squads](/wiki/Death_squad "Death squad") including [Sombra Negra](/wiki/Sombra_Negra "Sombra Negra").[[202]](#cite_note-208)

In 2015, there were 6,650 homicides recorded in El Salvador.[[203]](#cite_note-209) In 2016, at least 5,728 people were murdered.[[204]](#cite_note-210) In 2017, there were 3,962 recorded homicides.[[205]](#cite_note-211) In 2018, there were 3,340 recorded deaths.[[206]](#cite_note-212) In 2019, authorities reported a total of 2,365 homicides.[[207]](#cite_note-213) In 2020, there were only 1,322 reported homicides.[[208]](#cite_note-214) In 2021, the country recorded 1,140 homicides.[[209]](#cite_note-215) According to official data, 2021 had the lowest number of recorded murders since the end of the civil war in 1992.

By 2022, El Salvador had a homicide rate of 7.8 per 100,000 individuals.[[210]](#cite_note-216) On 10 May 2023, Bukele stated on Twitter that El Salvador had completed one full year or 365 days since 2019 without a single homicide occurring. This announcement was accompanied by a video detailing his government's claims about this dramatic change in the occurrences of homicide.[[211]](#cite_note-217) In 2024, El Salvador reported a homicide rate of 1.9 per 100,000 people, a figure lower than any other Latin American country.[[212]](#cite_note-218) This rate represents a 98% decrease in nine years.[[213]](#cite_note-Straight_Arrow_News-219)

#### 2022 crackdown on gangs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: 2022 crackdown on gangs")]

Main article: [2022 Salvadoran gang crackdown](/wiki/2022_Salvadoran_gang_crackdown "2022 Salvadoran gang crackdown")

Beginning on 25 March 2022, three days of gang-related violence occurred that left 87 people dead.[[214]](#cite_note-france24.com-220) In response, President Bukele asked the Salvadoran parliament to ratify a state of emergency.[[215]](#cite_note-221) On 26 March, Bukele also ordered the police and army to initiate mass-arrests against those responsible for the violence.

A day later, [Congress](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_El_Salvador "Legislative Assembly of El Salvador") approved a "[State of Emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency")" that gives legal coverage to arrest any citizen suspected to be a gang member even with no proof. In addition, Congress also approved reforms to increase the maximum sentence for gang membership from 9 to 45 years in prison and punish the dissemination of gang messages, including independent journalism talking about the gang crisis, with up to 15 years in prison.

The law was directed against those who "mark" their territories with acronyms of the gangs, a practice that gang members use to intimidate, and threaten with death those who denounce them to the authorities. The Directorate of Penal Centres began to erase the graffiti that the gangs use to mark the territory in which they operate.

The [Mara Salvatrucha (MS-13)](/wiki/MS-13 "MS-13") and [Barrio 18 gangs](/wiki/18th_Street_gang "18th Street gang"), among others, were estimated in 2022 to have around some 70,000 members, and as of August 2023, around 72,000 suspected gang members have been sent to prison as a part of the government crackdown on the gangs.[[216]](#cite_note-222)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of El Salvador](/wiki/Culture_of_El_Salvador "Culture of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/SalvadorDelMundo.jpg/250px-SalvadorDelMundo.jpg)](/wiki/File:SalvadorDelMundo.jpg)

The iconic statue of Christ on the globe sphere of planet earth is part of the [Monumento al Divino Salvador del Mundo](/wiki/Monumento_al_Divino_Salvador_del_Mundo "Monumento al Divino Salvador del Mundo") ('Monument to the Divine Saviour of the World') on Plaza El Salvador del Mundo ('The Saviour of the World Plaza'), a landmark located in El Salvador's capital, San Salvador.

Pulling from indigenous, colonial Spanish and African influences, a composite population was formed as a result of intermarrying between the natives, European settlers, and enslaved Africans. The Catholic Church plays an important role in the Salvadoran culture. Archbishop [Óscar Romero](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Romero "Óscar Romero") is a national hero for his role in resisting human rights violations that were occurring in the lead-up to the Salvadoran Civil War.[[217]](#cite_note-Eaton-223) Significant foreign personalities in El Salvador were the Jesuit priests and professors [Ignacio Ellacuría](/wiki/Ignacio_Ellacur%C3%ADa "Ignacio Ellacuría"), [Ignacio Martín-Baró](/wiki/Ignacio_Mart%C3%ADn-Bar%C3%B3 "Ignacio Martín-Baró"), and [Segundo Montes](/wiki/Segundo_Montes "Segundo Montes"), who were murdered in 1989 by the Salvadoran Army during the height of the civil war.

Painting, ceramics and textiles are the principal manual artistic mediums. Writers [Francisco Gavidia](/wiki/Francisco_Gavidia "Francisco Gavidia"), [Salarrué](/wiki/Salarru%C3%A9 "Salarrué") (Salvador Salazar Arrué), [Claudia Lars](/wiki/Claudia_Lars "Claudia Lars"), [Alfredo Espino](/wiki/Alfredo_Espino "Alfredo Espino"), [Pedro Geoffroy Rivas](/wiki/Pedro_Geoffroy_Rivas "Pedro Geoffroy Rivas"), [Manlio Argueta](/wiki/Manlio_Argueta "Manlio Argueta"), [José Roberto Cea](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Roberto_Cea "José Roberto Cea"), and poet [Roque Dalton](/wiki/Roque_Dalton "Roque Dalton") are important writers from El Salvador. Notable 20th-century personages include the late filmmaker Baltasar Polio, female film director [Patricia Chica](/wiki/Patricia_Chica "Patricia Chica"), artist [Fernando Llort](/wiki/Fernando_Llort "Fernando Llort"), and [caricaturist](/wiki/Caricaturist "Caricaturist") [Toño Salazar](/wiki/To%C3%B1o_Salazar "Toño Salazar").

Among the more renowned representatives of the graphic arts are the painters [Studio Lenca](/wiki/Studio_Lenca "Studio Lenca"), [Augusto Crespin](/wiki/Augusto_Crespin "Augusto Crespin"), [Noe Canjura](/wiki/Noe_Canjura "Noe Canjura"), [Carlos Cañas](/wiki/Carlos_Ca%C3%B1as "Carlos Cañas"), [Giovanni Gil](/wiki/Giovanni_Gil "Giovanni Gil"), Julia Díaz, Mauricio Mejia, Maria Elena Palomo de Mejia, [Camilo Minero](/wiki/Camilo_Minero "Camilo Minero"), Ricardo Carbonell, Roberto Huezo, Miguel Angel Cerna, (the painter and writer better known as MACLo), Esael Araujo, and many others.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of El Salvador](/wiki/Music_of_El_Salvador "Music of El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Debut_de_la_Compa%C3%B1ia_Infantil_de_Teatro_La_Colmenita_de_El_Salvador._%2824055587303%29.jpg/250px-Debut_de_la_Compa%C3%B1ia_Infantil_de_Teatro_La_Colmenita_de_El_Salvador._%2824055587303%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Debut_de_la_Compa%C3%B1ia_Infantil_de_Teatro_La_Colmenita_de_El_Salvador._(24055587303).jpg)

[Álvaro Torres](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Torres "Álvaro Torres") is one of the most famous Salvadoran singers mainly in the Latin pop genre, romantic ballads and boleros.

Traditional Salvadoran music is a mixture of indigenous, Spanish, and African influences. It includes religious songs (mostly used to celebrate Christmas and other holidays, especially feast days of the saints). Other musical repertoire consists of danza, pasillo, marcha and cancione which are composed of parading bands, street performances, or onstage dances, either in groups or paired. Satirical and rural lyrical themes are common. Traditional instruments used are the *[marimba](/wiki/Marimba "Marimba")*, *tepehuaste*, flutes, drums, [scrapers](/wiki/G%C3%BCiro "Güiro") and [gourds](/wiki/Gourd "Gourd"), as well as guitars among others. El Salvador's well known folk dance is known as *[Xuc](/wiki/Xuc "Xuc")* which originated in [Cojutepeque](/wiki/Cojutepeque "Cojutepeque"), Cuscatlan. Caribbean, Colombian, and Mexican music has become customary listening radio and party in the country, especially boleros, cumbia, merengue, Latin pop, salsa, bachata, and reggaeton.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Salvadoran cuisine](/wiki/Salvadoran_cuisine "Salvadoran cuisine")

One of El Salvador's notable dishes is the *[pupusa](/wiki/Pupusa "Pupusa")*. *Pupusas* are handmade [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") tortillas (made of *[masa de maíz](/wiki/Masa "Masa")* or *masa de arroz*, a maize or rice flour dough used in [Latin American cuisine](/wiki/Latin_American_cuisine "Latin American cuisine")) stuffed with one or more of the following: cheese (usually a soft Salvadoran cheese such as *[quesillo](/wiki/Quesillo "Quesillo")*, similar to mozzarella), *[chicharrón](/wiki/Chicharr%C3%B3n "Chicharrón")*, or [refried beans](/wiki/Refried_beans "Refried beans"). Sometimes the filling is *queso con loroco* (cheese combined with *[loroco](/wiki/Loroco "Loroco")*, a vine flower bud native to Central America).[[218]](#cite_note-Elsalvador.com-224) *Pupusas revueltas* are *pupusas* filled with beans, cheese and pork. There are also vegetarian options. Some adventurous restaurants even offer *pupusas* stuffed with shrimp or spinach. The name *pupusa* comes from the Pipil-Nahuatl word, *pupushahua*. The origins of the *pupusa* are debated, although its presence in El Salvador is known to predate the arrival of the Spaniards.[[218]](#cite_note-Elsalvador.com-224)

In El Salvador, the pupusa is considered a Mesoamerican ancestral legacy and the most popular dish nationally. It has been designated as the "National Dish of El Salvador" via the Legislative Decree no. 655 in the Salvadoran Constitution. The decree also indicates that every second Sunday in November, the country will celebrate the "National Day of the Pupusas".[[219]](#cite_note-225)

Two other typical Salvadoran dishes are *yuca frita* and *panes con pollo*. *Yuca frita* is deep fried [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava") root served with [curtido](/wiki/Curtido "Curtido") (a pickled cabbage, onion and carrot topping) and pork rinds with *pescaditas* (fried baby sardines). Yuca is sometimes served boiled instead of fried. *Pan con pollo/pavo* (bread with chicken/turkey) are warm turkey or chicken-filled submarine sandwiches. The bird is marinated and then roasted with spices and hand-pulled. This sandwich is traditionally served with tomato and watercress along with cucumber, onion, lettuce, mayonnaise, and mustard.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Plain_pupusas_revueltas.jpg/250px-Plain_pupusas_revueltas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plain_pupusas_revueltas.jpg)

*Pupusas*, the national and most famous dish of El Salvador

One of El Salvador's typical breakfasts is fried plantain, usually served with cream. It is common in Salvadoran restaurants and homes, including those of immigrants to the United States. [Alguashte](/wiki/Alguashte "Alguashte"), a condiment made from dried, ground [pepitas](/wiki/Pepitas "Pepitas"), is commonly incorporated into savoury and sweet Salvadoran dishes. "*Maria Luisa*" is a dessert commonly found in El Salvador. It is a layered cake that is soaked in orange marmalade and sprinkled with powdered sugar. One of the most popular desserts is the cake *Pastel de tres leches* (Cake of three milks), consisting of three types of milk: evaporated milk, condensed milk, and cream.

A popular drink that Salvadorans enjoy is *[horchata](/wiki/Horchata "Horchata")*. *Horchata* is most commonly made of the [morro seed](/wiki/Morro_seed "Morro seed") ground into a powder and added to milk or water, and sugar. *Horchata* is drunk year-round, and can be drunk at any time of day. It mostly is accompanied by a plate of *pupusas* or fried yuca. *Horchata* from El Salvador has a very distinct taste and is not to be confused with Mexican *horchata*, which is rice-based. Coffee is also a common morning beverage. Other popular drinks in El Salvador include *ensalada*, a drink made of chopped fruit swimming in fruit juice, and *Kolachampan*, a sugar cane-flavoured carbonated beverage.

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=El_Salvador&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in El Salvador](/wiki/Sport_in_El_Salvador "Sport in El Salvador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Monumental_Estadio_Cuscatlan.jpg/250px-Monumental_Estadio_Cuscatlan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monumental_Estadio_Cuscatlan.jpg)

The [Estadio Cuscatlán](/wiki/Estadio_Cuscatl%C3%A1n "Estadio Cuscatlán") in San Salvador is the largest stadium in Central America.

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in El Salvador. The [El Salvador national football team](/wiki/El_Salvador_national_football_team "El Salvador national football team") qualified for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [1970](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") and [1982](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"). Their qualification for the 1970 tournament was marred by the [Football War](/wiki/Football_War "Football War"), a war against [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), whose team El Salvador's had defeated. The national football team play at the [Estadio Cuscatlán](/wiki/Estadio_Cuscatl%C3%A1n "Estadio Cuscatlán") in San Salvador. It opened in 1976 and seats 53,400, making it the largest stadium in Central America and the Caribbean.[[220]](#cite_note-226)

* ["Background Notes"](https://2009-2017.state.gov/r/pa/ei/bgn/2033.htm), *Background Notes: El Salvador*, January 2008. Retrieved 6 March 2008.
* Bonner, Raymond. *Weakness and Deceit: U.S. Policy and El Salvador*. New York: Times Books, 1984.
* [*CIA World Factbook*](https://web.archive.org/web/20260114021956/https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/el-salvador/), "El Salvador", 28 February 2008. Retrieved 6 March 2008.
* ["Country Specific Information"](https://web.archive.org/web/20100510070619/http://travel.state.gov/travel/cis_pa_tw/cis/cis_1109.html), *U.S. State Department*, 3 October 2007. Retrieved 6 March 2008.
* Danner, Mark. *The Massacre at El Mozote: A Parable of the Cold War*. New York: Vintage Books, 1994.
* Foley, Erin. 'Cultures of the world, El Salvador. 1995
* Montgomery, Tommie Sue. *Revolution in El Salvador: From Civil Strife to Civil Peace*. Boulder, Colorado: Westview Press, 1995.
* Rosa, Audrey Celeste (1998). [*The courage to change: Salvadoran stories of personal and social transformation (El Salvador)*](http://scholars.wlu.ca/etd/661/) (M.A. thesis). Wilfrid Laurier University.
* Stadler, Sidney. *It Started with an Oyster: The Memoirs of Sidney M. Stadler, CBE*. Penna Press 1975. Autobiography of a British businessman and diplomat in El Salvador, with much on Salvadoran society and politics from the 1920s to 1950s.
* Vilas, Carlos. *Between Earthquakes and Volcanoes: Market, State, and the Revolution America*. New York: Monthly Review Press. 1995.
